sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAP-C02 1.1 to determine the required depth of the answer.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to the BGP session.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that shift risk from ASN design to route preference without actually addressing it.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./examFocus/3: Select an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence from path advertisement.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/title: Architecture review: BGP, ASN, and hybrid routing policies
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because two data centers advertise the same prefix, but traffic follows the wrong path and failover does not align with business priorities. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs a cost commitment.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. Your role is to translate requirements into decisions and clearly communicate trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are BGP session → ASN design → route preference → path advertisement.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the BGP session.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of ASN design.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations teams must be able to observe route preference without relying on assumptions.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off involving path advertisement.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the BGP session, ASN design, and route preference.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Map the request and data flow together with trust boundaries and the blast radius.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that do not satisfy mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow direction.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the BGP session and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or isolated keywords.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares ASN design, route preference, and path advertisement using the same criteria.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing priorities; no option is universally best.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the BGP session; ASN design is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option that prioritizes route preference is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of path advertisement without adding value.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the BGP session and ASN design and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which two data centers advertise the same prefix, but traffic follows the wrong path and failover does not align with business priorities.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reassess the decision?
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./recap/0: BGP, ASN, and hybrid routing policies must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./recap/1: The BGP session, ASN design, route preference, and path advertisement form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./recap/2: Every decision needs a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that do not satisfy mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of the BGP session and ASN design?
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode is associated with route preference, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./selfCheck/2: Why can path advertisement change the architectural choice?
sap-14-bgp-routing-policy./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?

sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./title: Hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./shortTitle: Hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./summary: Understand hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and applications across multiple accounts and on-premises environments need to resolve private names without creating duplicate resolvers. A diagram containing many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 15 starts with this scenario and turns hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces into an evidence-based sequence of decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces must be evaluated from four perspectives: private hosted zones, Resolver endpoints, forwarding rules, and DNS ownership. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or create unacceptable additional responsibilities.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 15—Hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces—is aligned with SAP-C02 objective 1.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic inventory is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must continue operating and the acceptable level of loss. The application describes traffic, data, and dependencies. The platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces, the private hosted zone is the entry point, the Resolver endpoint determines how the system responds, and forwarding rules and DNS ownership determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name usually causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that works for any lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A Requirement describes the outcome; a Constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a Decision is the selected architectural structure; and Evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. When R-C-D-E is applied to Hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but inappropriate for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 15 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundaries does the request cross? Which identity is evaluated? Where is the data retained? Which components can fail, and which mechanisms provide recovery? If the narrative stops at the private hosted zone or Resolver endpoint, the diagram still contains an unvalidated assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before adding another service.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/1/paragraphs/0: A private hosted zone is the first building block in decisions about hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the private hosted zone, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, applications across multiple accounts and on-premises environments need to resolve private names without creating duplicate resolvers. Connecting the private hosted zone to the Resolver endpoint clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A Resolver endpoint is the second building block in decisions about hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the Resolver endpoint, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, applications across multiple accounts and on-premises environments need to resolve private names without creating duplicate resolvers. Connecting the Resolver endpoint to the forwarding rule clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/1/paragraphs/2: A forwarding rule is the third building block in decisions about hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the forwarding rule, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, applications across multiple accounts and on-premises environments need to resolve private names without creating duplicate resolvers. Connecting the forwarding rule to DNS ownership clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/1/paragraphs/3: DNS ownership is the fourth building block in decisions about hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input governed by DNS ownership, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, applications across multiple accounts and on-premises environments need to resolve private names without creating duplicate resolvers. Connecting DNS ownership to the private hosted zone clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving a private hosted zone should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the private hosted zone with the forwarding rule using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 15, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a Resolver endpoint should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the Resolver endpoint with DNS ownership using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 15, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving a forwarding rule should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the forwarding rule with the private hosted zone using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 15, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving DNS ownership should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare DNS ownership with the Resolver endpoint using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 15, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces occurs when the project team assumes that the private hosted zone automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the private hosted zone, identifying the parts for which AWS is responsible and those the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces occurs when the project team assumes that the Resolver endpoint automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the Resolver endpoint, identifying the parts for which AWS is responsible and those the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces occurs when the project team assumes that the forwarding rule automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the forwarding rule, identifying the parts for which AWS is responsible and those the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 15, applications across multiple accounts and on-premises environments need to resolve private names without creating duplicate resolvers. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the private hosted zone, Resolver endpoint, and forwarding rule. Each option is scored using the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. DNS ownership serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces is written in four sentences: which structure is selected, which requirements it satisfies, which trade-off is accepted, and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/5/title: Thinking through actions in SAP-C02 questions
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces, mark every detail related to the private hosted zone and Resolver endpoint. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying, partially satisfying, introducing additional risk, or out of scope. Do not select an answer merely because it mentions the correct service. AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may call for a very different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 15, use the forwarding rule to verify the assurance level and DNS ownership to determine whether the operations team can maintain the option. An answer that provides the correct feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 15—Hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces—SAP-C02 objective 1.1 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes. For determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option. For improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./visuals/0/title: Decision map: Hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces

sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision analysis for Hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the scenario in which “applications across multiple accounts and on-premises environments need to resolve private names without creating duplicate resolvers,” while still explaining the effects on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the private hosted zone and the responsibility of the Resolver endpoint.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to the forwarding rule.
The operations team must use actual data to observe and reassess DNS ownership.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the private hosted zone perspective in step 1.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address the Resolver endpoint by itself; the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the Resolver endpoint perspective in step 2.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address the forwarding rule by itself; the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the forwarding rule perspective in step 3.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address DNS ownership by itself; the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines a private hosted zone with a Resolver endpoint, uses the forwarding rule as a guardrail, and uses DNS ownership as operational evidence.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that do not satisfy mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare the options using the same quality attributes and the same time horizon.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would trigger reassessment of the decision.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize a private hosted zone when the primary requirement is addressed in step 1 of the decision.

sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/title: Architecture review: Hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because applications across multiple accounts and on-premises environments need to resolve private names without creating duplicate resolvers. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs a cost commitment.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into a decision and clearly communicate the trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are private hosted zone → Resolver endpoint → forwarding rule → DNS ownership.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the private hosted zone.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the Resolver endpoint.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the forwarding rule without relying on assumptions.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off related to DNS ownership.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the private hosted zone, Resolver endpoint, and forwarding rule.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Map the request or data flow together with trust boundaries and the blast radius.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow direction.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the private hosted zone and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or a single keyword.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the Resolver endpoint, forwarding rule, and DNS ownership using the same criteria.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing competing concerns; no option is universally best.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, metric, or validation test.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the private hosted zone; the Resolver endpoint is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option that prioritizes the forwarding rule is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of DNS ownership without adding value.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines a private hosted zone and Resolver endpoint, with a clearly documented blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which applications across multiple accounts and on-premises environments need to resolve private names without creating duplicate resolvers.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirements.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/commonMistakes/2: Claiming that “AWS manages it automatically” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reassess the decision?
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./recap/0: Hybrid DNS, Resolver endpoints, and private namespaces must start with requirements and constraints.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./recap/1: The private hosted zone, Resolver endpoint, forwarding rule, and DNS ownership form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./recap/2: Every decision needs a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-15-hybrid-dns-architecture./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.

sap-16-privatelink-service-network./summary: Understand PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and a platform service must serve hundreds of VPCs with overlapping CIDR ranges without enabling bidirectional routing. A diagram filled with AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 16 begins with this scenario and turns PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery must be evaluated from four perspectives: the endpoint service, interface endpoint, consumer VPC, and overlapping CIDR. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 16—PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 1.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must continue operating and what level of loss is acceptable; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery, the endpoint service is the entry point, the interface endpoint indicates how the system responds, and the consumer VPC and overlapping CIDR determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that applies to every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You understand Lesson 16 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundaries does the request cross? Which identities are evaluated? Where is the data retained? Which components can fail, and which mechanisms provide recovery? If the story stops at the endpoint service or interface endpoint, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The endpoint service is the first building block in a PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery decision, but its value becomes clear only in the context of business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what the service does. Identify the input received by the endpoint service, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, a platform service must serve hundreds of VPCs with overlapping CIDR ranges without enabling bidirectional routing. Connecting the endpoint service to the interface endpoint reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The interface endpoint is the second building block in a PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery decision, but its value becomes clear only in the context of business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what the service does. Identify the input received by the interface endpoint, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, a platform service must serve hundreds of VPCs with overlapping CIDR ranges without enabling bidirectional routing. Connecting the interface endpoint to the consumer VPC reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The consumer VPC is the third building block in a PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery decision, but its value becomes clear only in the context of business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what the service does. Identify the input received by the consumer VPC, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, a platform service must serve hundreds of VPCs with overlapping CIDR ranges without enabling bidirectional routing. Connecting the consumer VPC to the overlapping CIDR reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/1/paragraphs/3: The overlapping CIDR is the fourth building block in a PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery decision, but its value becomes clear only in the context of business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what the service does. Identify the input received by the overlapping CIDR, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, a platform service must serve hundreds of VPCs with overlapping CIDR ranges without enabling bidirectional routing. Connecting the overlapping CIDR to the endpoint service reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving an endpoint service should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm the mandatory requirements. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the endpoint service with the consumer VPC using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 16, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving an interface endpoint should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm the mandatory requirements. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the interface endpoint with the overlapping CIDR using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 16, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving a consumer VPC should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm the mandatory requirements. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the consumer VPC with the endpoint service using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 16, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving overlapping CIDR should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm the mandatory requirements. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare overlapping CIDR with the interface endpoint using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 16, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery occurs when the project team assumes that the endpoint service automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the endpoint service, marking the areas for which AWS is responsible and those the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery occurs when the project team assumes that the interface endpoint automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the interface endpoint, marking the areas for which AWS is responsible and those the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery occurs when the project team assumes that the consumer VPC automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the consumer VPC, marking the areas for which AWS is responsible and those the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and operations teams working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 16, a platform service must serve hundreds of VPCs with overlapping CIDR ranges without enabling bidirectional routing. The first step is to distinguish the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and product teams need to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the endpoint service, interface endpoint, and consumer VPC. Each option is scored against the same matrix: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of consumption. The overlapping CIDR serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not satisfy a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/5/title: Choosing the correct action in SAP-C02 questions
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, required outcome, constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery, mark every detail related to the endpoint service and interface endpoint. Then classify each answer as fully compliant, partially compliant, introducing additional risk, or out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service. AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful scrutiny. They may require a very different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 16, use the consumer VPC to validate the assurance level and overlapping CIDR to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 16—PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery—objective SAP-C02 1.1 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./visuals/0/title: Decision map: PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to the endpoint service, interface endpoint, consumer VPC, and overlapping CIDR in the BlueRetail case.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: PrivateLink and cross-account service delivery decision analysis
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the scenario in which “a platform service must serve hundreds of VPCs with overlapping CIDR ranges without enabling bidirectional routing,” while still being able to explain the effects on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the endpoint service boundary and the responsibilities of the interface endpoint.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to the consumer VPC.
The operations team must observe and reassess overlapping CIDR using real data.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the endpoint service perspective in step 1.

sap-16-privatelink-service-network./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the interface endpoint perspective in step 2.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address the consumer VPC by itself; the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-16-privatelink-service-network./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the consumer VPC perspective in step 3.

sap-16-privatelink-service-network./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines the endpoint service with the interface endpoint, using the consumer VPC as a guardrail and overlapping CIDR as operational evidence.

You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs cost commitments.

sap-16-privatelink-service-network./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The response identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validation metric or test.

sap-17-global-traffic-management./title: Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic
sap-17-global-traffic-management./shortTitle: Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic
sap-17-global-traffic-management./summary: Understand Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and its multi-region application must redirect users quickly during failures while maintaining appropriate latency for each market. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 17 starts with this scenario to turn Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic should be considered from four perspectives: global endpoint, health routing, anycast address, and traffic policy. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if they conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 17—Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 2.4 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic, the global endpoint is the entry point, health routing determines how the system responds, and the anycast address and traffic policy determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for any lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the desired outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/0/paragraphs/2: A sign that you understand Lesson 17 is that you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data stored, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the global endpoint or health routing, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The global endpoint is the first building block in decisions about Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input the global endpoint receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the multi-region application must redirect users quickly during failures while maintaining appropriate latency for each market. Connecting the global endpoint to health routing reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Health routing is the second building block in decisions about Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input health routing receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the multi-region application must redirect users quickly during failures while maintaining appropriate latency for each market. Connecting health routing to the anycast address reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The anycast address is the third building block in decisions about Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input the anycast address receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the multi-region application must redirect users quickly during failures while maintaining appropriate latency for each market. Connecting the anycast address to the traffic policy reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/1/paragraphs/3: The traffic policy is the fourth building block in decisions about Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input the traffic policy receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the multi-region application must redirect users quickly during failures while maintaining appropriate latency for each market. Connecting the traffic policy to the global endpoint reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving a global endpoint should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the global endpoint with the anycast address using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 17, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context while also identifying the signals that would trigger an architectural review.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving health routing should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare health routing with the traffic policy using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 17, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context while also identifying the signals that would trigger an architectural review.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving an anycast address should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the anycast address with the global endpoint using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 17, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context while also identifying the signals that would trigger an architectural review.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving a traffic policy should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the traffic policy with health routing using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 17, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context while also identifying the signals that would trigger an architectural review.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic occurs when the project team assumes that the global endpoint automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the global endpoint, identify the portion for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic occurs when the project team assumes that health routing automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of health routing, identify the portion for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic occurs when the project team assumes that the anycast address automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the anycast address, identify the portion for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 17, the multi-Region application must redirect users quickly during failures while maintaining appropriate latency for each market. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a consistent experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the global endpoint, health routing, and the anycast address. Each option is evaluated against the same scorecard: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. The traffic policy serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the team must revisit the constraints.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it meets; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reevaluation. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/5/title: Choosing the right action in SAP-C02 questions
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic, mark every detail related to the global endpoint and health routing. Then classify each answer as fully meeting the requirements, partially meeting them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not select an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may require a very different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 17, use the anycast address to assess the required assurance level and the traffic policy to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 17—Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic—objective SAP-C02 2.4 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./visuals/0/title: Decision map: Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic
sap-17-global-traffic-management./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to the global endpoint, health routing, anycast address, and traffic policy in the BlueRetail case.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision analysis for Route 53, Global Accelerator, and global traffic
sap-17-global-traffic-management./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the scenario in which “a multi-Region application must redirect users quickly during failures while maintaining appropriate latency for each market,” while still being able to explain the effects on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the global endpoint and the responsibility of health routing.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to the anycast address.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reevaluate the traffic policy.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the global endpoint perspective in step 1.

sap-17-global-traffic-management./comparison/0/tradeOff: Health routing must be added or verified; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize health routing when the primary requirement is addressed in decision step 2.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses health routing to address a scenario in which a multi-region application must redirect users quickly during failures while maintaining appropriate latency for each market.

sap-17-global-traffic-management./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the anycast address to address a scenario in which a multi-region application must redirect users quickly during failures while maintaining appropriate latency for each market.

sap-17-global-traffic-management./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because its multi-region application must redirect users quickly during failures while maintaining appropriate latency for each market. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs cost commitments.

sap-17-global-traffic-management./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the global endpoint.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of health routing.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations teams must be able to observe the anycast address without relying on assumptions.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal an inadequately defined requirement.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off involving the traffic policy.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.

sap-17-global-traffic-management./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.

sap-17-global-traffic-management./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Health routing, the anycast address, and the traffic policy are compared using the same criteria.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing priorities; there is no universally best option.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-17-global-traffic-management./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
