sap-21-kms-cloudhsm-key-governance./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to verify coverage—Lesson 21

sap-22-data-classification-protection./summary: Understand data classification and protective controls through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and BlueRetail does not know which buckets contain personal data, so it cannot apply retention policies and access permissions based on sensitivity. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it effectively. Lesson 22 starts with this scenario and turns data classification and protective controls into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Data classification and protective controls should be evaluated from four perspectives: data classification, Macie discovery, encryption boundary, and retention control. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if they conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 22 — Data Classification and Protective Controls is aligned with objective SAP-C02 2.3 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/0/title: Mental Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must continue operating and the acceptable level of loss; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For data classification and protective controls, data classification is the entry point, Macie discovery shows how the system responds, and the encryption boundary and retention control determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that can be used for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to Data Classification and Protective Controls, learners will see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but unsuitable for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 22 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at data classification or Macie discovery, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Be Connected
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/1/paragraphs/0: data classification is the 1st component in a decision about data classification and protective controls, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by data classification, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, BlueRetail does not know which buckets contain personal data, so it cannot apply retention policies and access permissions based on sensitivity. Connecting data classification with Macie discovery reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Macie discovery is the 2nd component in a decision about data classification and protective controls, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by Macie discovery, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, BlueRetail does not know which buckets contain personal data, so it cannot apply retention policies and access permissions based on sensitivity. Connecting Macie discovery with the encryption boundary reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/1/paragraphs/2: encryption boundary is the 3rd component in a decision about data classification and protective controls, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the encryption boundary, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, BlueRetail does not know which buckets contain personal data, so it cannot apply retention policies and access permissions based on sensitivity. Connecting the encryption boundary with retention control reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/1/paragraphs/3: retention control is the 4th component in a decision about data classification and protective controls, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by retention control, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, BlueRetail does not know which buckets contain personal data, so it cannot apply retention policies and access permissions based on sensitivity. Connecting retention control with data classification reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Document the Trade-offs
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving data classification should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare data classification with the encryption boundary using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 22, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that would trigger an architectural review.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving Macie discovery should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare Macie discovery with retention control using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 22, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that would trigger an architectural review.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving the encryption boundary should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the encryption boundary with data classification using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 22, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that would trigger an architectural review.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving retention control should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare retention control with Macie discovery using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 22, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that would trigger an architectural review.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before It Occurs
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for data classification and protective controls occurs when the project team assumes that data classification automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of data classification, marking the part for which AWS is responsible and the part that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for data classification and protective controls occurs when the project team assumes that Macie discovery automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of Macie discovery, marking the part for which AWS is responsible and the part that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for data classification and protective controls occurs when the project team assumes that the encryption boundary automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the encryption boundary, marking the part for which AWS is responsible and the part that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 22, BlueRetail does not know which buckets contain personal data, so it cannot apply retention policies and access permissions based on sensitivity. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a consistent experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder will create operational debt for another.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on data classification, Macie discovery, and the encryption boundary. Each option is scored against the same criteria: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. retention control serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for data classification and protective controls is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reevaluation. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/5/title: Choosing the Right Action in SAP-C02 Questions
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the mandatory outcome, the constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For data classification and protective controls, mark every detail related to data classification and Macie discovery. Then classify the answers as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or falling outside the scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it names the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a completely different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 22, use the encryption boundary to evaluate the assurance level and retention control to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 22 — Data Classification and Protective Controls, objective SAP-C02 2.3 indicates the required depth. With the verb design, you must balance quality attributes; with determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option; with improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Data Classification and Protective Controls Decision Analysis
sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the situation in which “BlueRetail does not know which buckets contain personal data, so it cannot apply retention policies and access permissions based on sensitivity,” while still being able to explain the effects on security, recoverability, performance, and cost.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of data classification and the responsibility of Macie discovery.
The option must include a failure mode and a recovery path related to the encryption boundary.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reevaluate retention control.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the data classification perspective in step 1.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address Macie discovery by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the Macie discovery perspective in step 2.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address the encryption boundary by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the encryption boundary perspective in step 3.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address retention control by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines data classification with Macie discovery, uses the encryption boundary as a guardrail, and uses retention control as operational evidence.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that do not satisfy mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare the options using the same quality attributes and time horizon.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would trigger reevaluation of the decision.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize data classification when the primary requirement lies in step 1 of the decision.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./comparison/0/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses data classification to address the situation in which BlueRetail does not know which buckets contain personal data, so it cannot apply retention policies and access permissions based on sensitivity.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./comparison/0/tradeOff: Macie discovery must be added or evaluated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize Macie discovery when the primary requirement lies in step 2 of the decision.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses Macie discovery to address the situation in which BlueRetail does not know which buckets contain personal data, so it cannot apply retention policies and access permissions based on sensitivity.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./comparison/1/tradeOff: The encryption boundary must be added or evaluated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./comparison/2/bestFit: Prioritize the encryption boundary when the primary requirement lies in step 3 of the decision.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the encryption boundary to address the situation in which BlueRetail does not know which buckets contain personal data, so it cannot apply retention policies and access permissions based on sensitivity.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./comparison/2/tradeOff: retention control must be added or evaluated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the keyword data classification and immediately choosing the answer with the same service or feature name.

sap-22-data-classification-protection./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 22, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.

sap-22-data-classification-protection./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAP-C02 2.3 to determine the required depth of the answer.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to data classification.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that shift risk from Macie discovery to the encryption boundary without actually addressing it.

sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/title: Architecture Review: Data Classification and Protective Controls
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because BlueRetail does not know which buckets contain personal data, so it cannot apply retention policies and access permissions based on sensitivity. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs cost commitments.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly explain the trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the points to review are data classification → Macie discovery → encryption boundary → retention control.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to data classification.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of Macie discovery.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the encryption boundary without relying on assumptions.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving retention control must be accepted by stakeholders.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on data classification, Macie discovery, and the encryption boundary.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Map the request and data flows together with trust boundaries and the blast radius.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing the trade-off and supporting evidence.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to data classification and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or a single keyword.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Macie discovery, the encryption boundary, and retention control are compared using the same criteria.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always requires balance; there is no universally best option.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validation metric or test.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around data classification; Macie discovery is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option prioritizing the encryption boundary is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of retention control without adding value.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines data classification and Macie discovery and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which BlueRetail does not know which buckets contain personal data, so it cannot apply retention policies and access permissions based on sensitivity.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirements.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it automatically” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-22-data-classification-protection./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-22-data-classification-protection./recap/0: Data classification and protective controls must begin with requirements and constraints.

sap-22-data-classification-protection./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-22-data-classification-protection./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.

sap-23-edge-application-security./title: WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection
sap-23-edge-application-security./shortTitle: WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection
sap-23-edge-application-security./summary: Understand WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-23-edge-application-security./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and many public domains are being targeted by bots and DDoS attacks. Security needs shared policies, while each product must retain its own rules. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 23 begins with this scenario and turns WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection into an evidence-based decision process rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-23-edge-application-security./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection should be evaluated from four perspectives: web ACL, managed rule, Shield Advanced, and origin protection. Each perspective can eliminate a group of answers if they conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-23-edge-application-security./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 23—WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 2.3 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss. The application describes traffic, data, and dependencies. The platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection, the web ACL is the entry point, managed rules define how the system responds, and Shield Advanced and origin protection determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model applicable to every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the desired outcome. A constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills. A decision is the selected architectural structure. Evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. Applying R-C-D-E to WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection shows that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but unsuitable for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You understand Lesson 23 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross? Which identity is evaluated? Where is the data retained? Which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story stops at the web ACL or managed rule, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must work together
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The web ACL is the first component in a decision about WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the web ACL, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, many public domains are being targeted by bots and DDoS attacks. Security needs shared policies, while each product must retain its own rules. Connecting the web ACL to managed rules clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A managed rule is the second component in a decision about WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the managed rule, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, many public domains are being targeted by bots and DDoS attacks. Security needs shared policies, while each product must retain its own rules. Connecting the managed rule to Shield Advanced clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/1/paragraphs/2: Shield Advanced is the third component in a decision about WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by Shield Advanced, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, many public domains are being targeted by bots and DDoS attacks. Security needs shared policies, while each product must retain its own rules. Connecting Shield Advanced to origin protection clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Origin protection is the fourth component in a decision about WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by origin protection, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, many public domains are being targeted by bots and DDoS attacks. Security needs shared policies, while each product must retain its own rules. Connecting origin protection to the web ACL clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving a web ACL should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the web ACL with Shield Advanced using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 23, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would require the architecture to be reconsidered.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a managed rule should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the managed rule with origin protection using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 23, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would require the architecture to be reconsidered.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving Shield Advanced should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare Shield Advanced with the web ACL using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 23, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would require the architecture to be reconsidered.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving origin protection should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare origin protection with the managed rule using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 23, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would require the architecture to be reconsidered.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection occurs when the project team assumes that the web ACL automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the web ACL, identify what AWS is responsible for, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that an answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection occurs when the project team assumes that a managed rule automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the managed rule, identify what AWS is responsible for, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that an answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection occurs when the project team assumes that Shield Advanced automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of Shield Advanced, identify what AWS is responsible for, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that an answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 23, many public domains are being targeted by bots and DDoS attacks. Security needs shared policies, while each product must retain its own rules. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and product teams need to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on web ACL, managed rule, and Shield Advanced. Each option is scored using the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of use. Origin protection serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails to meet a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/5/title: Selecting the right action in SAP-C02 questions
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the final verb in the question. For WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection, mark every detail related to the web ACL and managed rule. Then classify the answers as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not select an answer merely because it mentions the correct service. AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may require a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 23, use Shield Advanced to evaluate the assurance level and origin protection to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-23-edge-application-security./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 23—WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection—objective SAP-C02 2.3 indicates the required depth of understanding. For design, you must balance quality attributes. For determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option. For improve, you must preserve existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision analysis for WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection
sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the situation in which “many public domains are being targeted by bots and DDoS attacks, while security needs shared policies but each product must retain its own rules,” and must still be able to explain the impact on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the web ACL and the responsibility of the managed rule.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to Shield Advanced.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess origin protection.
sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the web ACL perspective at step 1.
sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: It does not address the managed rule by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the managed rule perspective at step 2.
sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: It does not address Shield Advanced by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the Shield Advanced perspective at step 3.
sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: It does not address origin protection by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines a web ACL with managed rules, uses Shield Advanced as a guardrail, and uses origin protection as operational evidence.
sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare options using the same quality attributes and time horizon.
sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
sap-23-edge-application-security./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would require the decision to be reconsidered.
sap-23-edge-application-security./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize the web ACL when the primary requirement is at step 1 of the decision.
sap-23-edge-application-security./comparison/0/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses a web ACL to address the situation in which many public domains are being targeted by bots and DDoS attacks, while security needs shared policies but each product must retain its own rules.
sap-23-edge-application-security./comparison/0/tradeOff: Managed rules must be added or reviewed; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-23-edge-application-security./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize the managed rule when the primary requirement is at step 2 of the decision.
sap-23-edge-application-security./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses managed rules to address the situation in which many public domains are being targeted by bots and DDoS attacks, while security needs shared policies but each product must retain its own rules.
sap-23-edge-application-security./comparison/1/tradeOff: Shield Advanced must be added or reviewed; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-23-edge-application-security./comparison/2/bestFit: Prioritize Shield Advanced when the primary requirement is at step 3 of the decision.
sap-23-edge-application-security./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses Shield Advanced to address the situation in which many public domains are being targeted by bots and DDoS attacks, while security needs shared policies but each product must retain its own rules.
sap-23-edge-application-security./comparison/2/tradeOff: Origin protection must be added or reviewed; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.

sap-23-edge-application-security./commonMistakes/0/better: Evaluate the web ACL against the requirements, constraints, and managed rule before making a selection.

sap-23-edge-application-security./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAP-C02 2.3 to determine the required depth of the answer.
sap-23-edge-application-security./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to the web ACL.
sap-23-edge-application-security./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that shift risk from the managed rule to Shield Advanced without actually addressing it.
sap-23-edge-application-security./examFocus/3: Select an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence provided through origin protection.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/title: Architecture review: WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because many public domains are being targeted by bots and DDoS attacks, while security needs shared policies but each product must retain its own rules. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs a cost commitment.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly explain the trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are web ACL → managed rule → Shield Advanced → origin protection.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the web ACL.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the managed rule.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe Shield Advanced without relying on assumptions.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving origin protection must be accepted by stakeholders.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on web ACL, managed rule, and Shield Advanced.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flow, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail to meet mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/deliverables/2: A short architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the web ACL and introduces no assumptions beyond the question.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting a service based on preference or a single keyword.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares managed rule, Shield Advanced, and origin protection using the same criteria.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always requires balance; there is no universally best option.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validation metric or test.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the web ACL; the managed rule is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing Shield Advanced is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of origin protection without adding value.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines a web ACL and managed rules, with a clearly documented blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which many public domains are being targeted by bots and DDoS attacks, while security needs shared policies but each product must retain its own rules.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it automatically” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the question eliminated the most options?
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-23-edge-application-security./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-23-edge-application-security./recap/0: WAF, Shield Advanced, and global application protection must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-23-edge-application-security./recap/1: web ACL, managed rule, Shield Advanced, and origin protection form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-23-edge-application-security./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-23-edge-application-security./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail to meet mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-23-edge-application-security./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of a web ACL and a managed rule?
sap-23-edge-application-security./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode is related to Shield Advanced, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
sap-23-edge-application-security./selfCheck/2: Why can origin protection change the architectural choice?
sap-23-edge-application-security./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?
