sap-24-threat-detection-response./title: GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process
sap-24-threat-detection-response./shortTitle: GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process
sap-24-threat-detection-response./summary: Understand GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an explainable architectural decision for the BlueRetail team.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and a critical finding appears in a member account but is not enriched, assigned, or handled through a centralized runbook. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 24 starts with this scenario and turns GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process into an evidence-based chain of decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process should be considered from four perspectives: security findings, threat graphs, event routing, and incident response. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 24—GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 3.2 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/0/title: Thinking model: start with requirements, not services
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process, a security finding is the entry point, the threat graph shows how the system responds, and event routing and incident response determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that applies to every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. Applying R-C-D-E to GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process shows learners that the same service can be right for one workload and wrong for another. This skill matters more than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 24 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the security finding or threat graph, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before adding another service.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/1/paragraphs/0: A security finding is the 1st element in decisions about GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process, but it provides value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the security finding, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, a critical finding appears in a member account but is not enriched, assigned, or handled through a centralized runbook. Connecting the security finding to the threat graph reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A threat graph is the 2nd element in decisions about GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process, but it provides value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the threat graph, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, a critical finding appears in a member account but is not enriched, assigned, or handled through a centralized runbook. Connecting the threat graph to event routing reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/1/paragraphs/2: Event routing is the 3rd element in decisions about GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process, but it provides value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by event routing, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, a critical finding appears in a member account but is not enriched, assigned, or handled through a centralized runbook. Connecting event routing to incident response reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Incident response is the 4th element in decisions about GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process, but it provides value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by incident response, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, a critical finding appears in a member account but is not enriched, assigned, or handled through a centralized runbook. Connecting incident response to the security finding reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving a security finding should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the security finding with event routing using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total price of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 24, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals should trigger an architectural review.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a threat graph should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the threat graph with incident response using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total price of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 24, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals should trigger an architectural review.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving event routing should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare event routing with the security finding using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total price of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 24, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals should trigger an architectural review.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving incident response should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare incident response with the threat graph using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total price of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 24, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals should trigger an architectural review.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process occurs when the project team assumes that a security finding automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the security finding and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process occurs when the project team assumes that a threat graph automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the threat graph and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process occurs when the project team assumes that event routing automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of event routing and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 24, a critical finding appears in a member account but is not enriched, assigned, or handled through a centralized runbook. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the security finding, threat graph, and event routing. Each option is scored using the same matrix: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. Incident response serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the team must revisit the constraints.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger a reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/5/title: Choosing an action in SAP-C02 questions
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process, mark every detail related to the security finding and threat graph. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may require designs entirely different from those implied by “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “cost priority.” In Lesson 24, use event routing to verify the assurance level and incident response to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 24—GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process—objective SAP-C02 3.2 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./visuals/0/title: Decision map: GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process
sap-24-threat-detection-response./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting the requirement to the security finding, threat graph, event routing, and incident response in the BlueRetail case.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision analysis for GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the situation in which “a critical finding appears in a member account but is not enriched, assigned, or handled through a centralized runbook,” while still being able to explain the impact on security, recoverability, performance, and cost.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clearly define the security finding boundary and the responsibility of the threat graph.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to event routing.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess incident response.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the security finding perspective in step 1.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address the threat graph by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the threat graph perspective in step 2.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address event routing by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the event routing perspective in step 3.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address incident response by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines the security finding with the threat graph, uses event routing as a guardrail, and uses incident response as operational evidence.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare options using the same quality attributes and time horizon.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that should trigger a reassessment of the decision.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize the security finding when the primary requirement falls under step 1 of the decision.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./comparison/0/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the security finding to address the situation in which a critical finding appears in a member account but is not enriched, assigned, or handled through a centralized runbook.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./comparison/0/tradeOff: The threat graph must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize the threat graph when the primary requirement falls under step 2 of the decision.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the threat graph to address the situation in which a critical finding appears in a member account but is not enriched, assigned, or handled through a centralized runbook.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./comparison/1/tradeOff: Event routing must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./comparison/2/bestFit: Prioritize event routing when the primary requirement falls under step 3 of the decision.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses event routing to address the situation in which a critical finding appears in a member account but is not enriched, assigned, or handled through a centralized runbook.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./comparison/2/tradeOff: Incident response must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.

sap-24-threat-detection-response./commonMistakes/0/better: Evaluate the security finding against the requirement, constraint, and threat graph before choosing.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 24, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.

sap-24-threat-detection-response./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the keyword event routing and immediately choosing an answer with a matching service or feature name.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./commonMistakes/2/better: Evaluate event routing against the requirement, constraint, and incident response before choosing.

sap-24-threat-detection-response./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to the security finding.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that shift risk from the threat graph to event routing without actually addressing it.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./examFocus/3: Choose an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence from incident response.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/title: Architecture review: GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because a critical finding appears in a member account but is not enriched, assigned, or handled through a centralized runbook. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs a cost commitment.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly communicate trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are security finding → threat graph → event routing → incident response.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the security finding.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the threat graph.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations teams must be able to observe event routing without relying on assumptions.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the incident response trade-off.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the security finding, threat graph, and event routing.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unconfirmed assumptions.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Map the request and data flows together with trust boundaries and blast radius.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow directions.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/deliverables/2: A short architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the security finding and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or isolated keywords.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the threat graph, event routing, and incident response using the same criteria.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always requires balance; no option is absolutely best.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validation metric or test.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the security finding; the threat graph is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing event routing is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of incident response without adding value.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the security finding and threat graph and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which a critical finding appears in a member account but is not enriched, assigned, or handled through a centralized runbook.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer’s responsibilities.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-24-threat-detection-response./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-24-threat-detection-response./recap/0: GuardDuty, Detective, and the response process must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./recap/1: The security finding, threat graph, event routing, and incident response form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./recap/2: Every decision needs a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-24-threat-detection-response./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of a security finding and a threat graph?
sap-24-threat-detection-response./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode is related to event routing, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
sap-24-threat-detection-response./selfCheck/2: Why can incident response change the architectural choice?
sap-24-threat-detection-response./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?

sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./title: Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./shortTitle: Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./summary: Understand Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and the company must demonstrate that controls operate continuously instead of collecting configuration snapshots at the end of the audit period. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 25 starts with this scenario and turns Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager into a chain of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one is the best fit for keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager should be evaluated from four perspectives: configuration rule, security standard, control evidence, and compliance aggregation. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if it conflicts with a requirement or creates an unacceptable additional responsibility.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 25—Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 3.2 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are cross-checked against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business specifies what must continue operating and the acceptable level of loss; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager, the configuration rule is the entry point, the security standard defines how the system responds, and control evidence and compliance aggregation determine long-term operability. If you jump directly from a requirement to a service name, you will often miss one of these four layers.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for any lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but inappropriate for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 25 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the configuration rule or security standard, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before adding another service.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The configuration rule is the first building block in a Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the configuration rule, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the company must demonstrate that controls operate continuously instead of collecting configuration snapshots at the end of the audit period. Connecting the configuration rule to the security standard reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The security standard is the second building block in a Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the security standard, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the company must demonstrate that controls operate continuously instead of collecting configuration snapshots at the end of the audit period. Connecting the security standard to control evidence reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/1/paragraphs/2: Control evidence is the third building block in a Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by control evidence, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the company must demonstrate that controls operate continuously instead of collecting configuration snapshots at the end of the audit period. Connecting control evidence to compliance aggregation reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Compliance aggregation is the fourth building block in a Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by compliance aggregation, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the company must demonstrate that controls operate continuously instead of collecting configuration snapshots at the end of the audit period. Connecting compliance aggregation to the configuration rule reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving a configuration rule should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the configuration rule with control evidence using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 25, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that would require the architecture to be reconsidered.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a security standard should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the security standard with compliance aggregation using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 25, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that would require the architecture to be reconsidered.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving control evidence should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare control evidence with the configuration rule using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 25, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that would require the architecture to be reconsidered.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving compliance aggregation should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare compliance aggregation with the security standard using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 25, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that would require the architecture to be reconsidered.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager occurs when the project team assumes that the configuration rule automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the configuration rule and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager occurs when the project team assumes that the security standard automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the security standard and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager occurs when the project team assumes that control evidence automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of control evidence and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 25, the company must demonstrate that controls operate continuously instead of collecting configuration snapshots at the end of the audit period. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the configuration rule, security standard, and control evidence. Each option is scored against the same matrix: mandatory requirement coverage, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. Compliance aggregation serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the team must revisit the constraint.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger a reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not meet a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/5/title: Thinking through actions in SAP-C02 questions
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraint, and the verb in the final sentence. For Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager, mark every detail related to the configuration rule and security standard. Then classify the answers as fully meeting the requirements, partially meeting them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it names the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the best-fit solution.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may require a very different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 25, use control evidence to verify the level of assurance and compliance aggregation to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that is functionally correct but provides the wrong level of assurance is still a distractor.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 25—Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager—objective SAP-C02 3.2 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with a form of evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./visuals/0/title: Decision map: Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting the requirement to the configuration rule, security standard, control evidence, and compliance aggregation in the BlueRetail case.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager decision analysis
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the scenario in which “the company must demonstrate that controls operate continuously instead of collecting configuration snapshots at the end of the audit period,” while still being able to explain the effects on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the configuration rule and the responsibility of the security standard.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to control evidence.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess compliance aggregation.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the configuration rule perspective in step 1.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address the security standard by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.

sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./activity/title: Architecture review: Config, Security Hub, and Audit Manager
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the company must demonstrate that controls operate continuously instead of collecting configuration snapshots at the end of the audit period. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs a cost commitment.

sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving compliance aggregation must be accepted by stakeholders.
sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.

sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.

sap-25-config-securityhub-auditmanager./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The work identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validation metric or test.

sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./title: Responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./shortTitle: Responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./summary: Understand responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an explainable architecture decision for the BlueRetail team.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and its AI assistant can access internal data and trigger actions, so it requires content filtering, a separate identity, and a human approval step. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it effectively. Lesson 26 begins with this scenario and turns responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight should be evaluated from four perspectives: Bedrock Guardrails, AgentCore Identity, human approval, and responsible AI control. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if they conflict with the requirements or create unacceptable additional responsibilities.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 26—Responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight—is aligned with the SAP-C02 Emerging Topics objective in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic inventory is used only to check coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight, Bedrock Guardrails is the entry point, AgentCore Identity determines how the system responds, and human approval and responsible AI control determine long-term operability. Moving directly from requirements to service names often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architecture structure; and evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to Responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight, learners can see that the same service may be correct for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/0/paragraphs/2: A sign that you understand Lesson 26 is that you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundaries does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which components can fail, and which mechanisms provide recovery? If the story breaks at Bedrock Guardrails or AgentCore Identity, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting additional services.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must work together
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Bedrock Guardrails is the first component of the responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight decision, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input Bedrock Guardrails receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the AI assistant can access internal data and trigger actions, so it requires content filtering, a separate identity, and a human approval step. Connecting Bedrock Guardrails with AgentCore Identity clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/1/paragraphs/1: AgentCore Identity is the second component of the responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight decision, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input AgentCore Identity receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. Connecting AgentCore Identity with human approval clarifies the responsibility boundary and explains why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving Bedrock Guardrails should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare Bedrock Guardrails with human approval using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 26, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving AgentCore Identity should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare AgentCore Identity with responsible AI control using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 26, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.

Next, compare human approval with Bedrock Guardrails using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight occurs when the project team assumes that Bedrock Guardrails automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of Bedrock Guardrails and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight occurs when the project team assumes that AgentCore Identity automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of AgentCore Identity and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight occurs when the project team assumes that human approval automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of human approval and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 26, the AI assistant can access internal data and trigger actions, so it requires content filtering, a separate identity, and a human approval step. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on Bedrock Guardrails, AgentCore Identity, and human approval. Each option is scored against the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of consumption. responsible AI control serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-offs are accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reevaluation. This format helps developers, security, and operations share the same understanding of the decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/5/title: How to choose an action in SAP-C02 questions
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight, mark every detail related to Bedrock Guardrails and AgentCore Identity. Then classify each answer as fully compliant, partially compliant, introducing additional risk, or out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a very different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 26, use human approval to verify the required assurance level and responsible AI control to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the correct feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 26—Responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight—the SAP-C02 Emerging Topics objective indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architecture decision.

sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/title: Architecture review: Responsible AI: Bedrock Guardrails and human oversight
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the AI assistant can access internal data and trigger actions, so it requires content filtering, a separate identity, and a human approval step. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs cost commitments.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly communicate trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are Bedrock Guardrails → AgentCore Identity → human approval → responsible AI control.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to Bedrock Guardrails.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must show the role of AgentCore Identity.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe human approval without relying on assumptions.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving responsible AI control must be accepted by stakeholders.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on Bedrock Guardrails, AgentCore Identity, and human approval.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-offs, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flow, including trust boundaries and the blast radius.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-offs, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix comparing at least two options.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to Bedrock Guardrails and introduces no assumptions beyond the question.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or a single keyword.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares AgentCore Identity, human approval, and responsible AI control using the same criteria.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; there is no universally best option.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validation metric or test.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around Bedrock Guardrails; AgentCore Identity is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing human approval is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of responsible AI control without adding value.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines Bedrock Guardrails and AgentCore Identity and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-26-responsible-ai-controls./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which the AI assistant can access internal data and trigger actions, so it requires content filtering, a separate identity, and a human approval step.
