sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the keyword conflict resolution and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./commonMistakes/2/better: Compare conflict resolution against the requirement, constraints, and data sovereignty before selecting an answer.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./commonMistakes/2/reason: In Lesson 32, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibility and its failure modes are addressed.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAP-C02 2.4 to determine the required depth of the answer.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to replication topology.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that shift risk from the consistency model to conflict resolution without actually addressing it.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./examFocus/3: Select an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence provided through data sovereignty.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/title: Architecture Review: Multi-Region Data and Consistency Trade-offs
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because users write data in two Regions, but the conflict-handling rules and permitted data storage locations are unclear. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs cost commitments.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly explain the trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are replication topology → consistency model → conflict resolution → data sovereignty.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to replication topology.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the consistency model.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe conflict resolution without relying on assumptions.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to mask a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the data sovereignty trade-off.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on replication topology, consistency model, and conflict resolution.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request and data flows, including trust boundaries and blast radius.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data direction.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to replication topology and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting services based on preference or a single keyword.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares consistency model, conflict resolution, and data sovereignty using the same criteria.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always requires balancing trade-offs; no option is universally best.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around replication topology; consistency model is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option that prioritizes conflict resolution is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of data sovereignty without adding value.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines replication topology and consistency model and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which users write data in two Regions, but the conflict-handling rules and permitted data storage locations are unclear.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it automatically” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./recap/0: Multi-Region data and consistency trade-offs must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./recap/1: replication topology, consistency model, conflict resolution, and data sovereignty form a chain of responsibility.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./recap/2: Every decision requires trade-offs, a recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of replication topology and consistency model?
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode is related to conflict resolution, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./selfCheck/2: Why can data sovereignty change the architectural choice?
sap-32-multi-region-data-consistency./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?

sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./title: Large-Scale Scaling, Quotas, and Capacity Planning
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./shortTitle: Large-Scale Scaling, Quotas, and Capacity Planning
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./summary: Understand large-scale scaling, quotas, and capacity planning through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an explainable architectural decision for the BlueRetail team.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture when a sales event exceeds forecasts and a dependency reaches its quota before compute can scale. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 33 begins with this scenario and turns large-scale scaling, quotas, and capacity planning into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Large-scale scaling, quotas, and capacity planning must be examined from four perspectives: capacity model, service quota, autoscaling signal, and load test. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or create unacceptable additional responsibilities.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 33 — Large-Scale Scaling, Quotas, and Capacity Planning is aligned with objective SAP-C02 2.4 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/0/title: Mental Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must continue operating and what level of loss is acceptable; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For large-scale scaling, quotas, and capacity planning, the capacity model is the entry point, the service quota indicates how the system responds, and the autoscaling signal and load test determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model applicable to every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to large-scale scaling, quotas, and capacity planning, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but unsuitable for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 33 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the capacity model or service quota, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Be Connected
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/1/paragraphs/0: capacity model is the 1st component of a large-scale scaling, quota, and capacity planning decision, but its value emerges only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at the question, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by the capacity model, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, a sales event exceeds forecasts and a dependency reaches its quota before compute can scale. Connecting the capacity model to the service quota reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/1/paragraphs/1: service quota is the 2nd component of a large-scale scaling, quota, and capacity planning decision, but its value emerges only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at the question, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by the service quota, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, a sales event exceeds forecasts and a dependency reaches its quota before compute can scale. Connecting the service quota to the autoscaling signal reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/1/paragraphs/2: autoscaling signal is the 3rd component of a large-scale scaling, quota, and capacity planning decision, but its value emerges only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at the question, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by the autoscaling signal, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, a sales event exceeds forecasts and a dependency reaches its quota before compute can scale. Connecting the autoscaling signal to the load test reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/1/paragraphs/3: load test is the 4th component of a large-scale scaling, quota, and capacity planning decision, but its value emerges only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at the question, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by the load test, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, a sales event exceeds forecasts and a dependency reaches its quota before compute can scale. Connecting the load test to the capacity model reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Document the Trade-offs
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving the capacity model should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the capacity model with the autoscaling signal using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 33, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signal would trigger an architectural review.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving the service quota should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the service quota with the load test using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 33, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signal would trigger an architectural review.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving the autoscaling signal should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the autoscaling signal with the capacity model using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 33, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signal would trigger an architectural review.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving the load test should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the load test with the service quota using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 33, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signal would trigger an architectural review.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before It Occurs
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in large-scale scaling, quotas, and capacity planning occurs when the project team assumes that the capacity model automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the capacity model and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in large-scale scaling, quotas, and capacity planning occurs when the project team assumes that the service quota automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the service quota and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in large-scale scaling, quotas, and capacity planning occurs when the project team assumes that the autoscaling signal automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the autoscaling signal and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 33, a sales event exceeds forecasts and a dependency reaches its quota before compute can scale. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the capacity model, service quota, and autoscaling signal. Each option is scored against the same criteria: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. The load test serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for large-scale scaling, quotas, and capacity planning is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many appealing features.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/5/title: Choosing the Right Action in SAP-C02 Questions
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the final verb in the question. For large-scale scaling, quotas, and capacity planning, mark every detail related to the capacity model and service quota. Then classify the answers as fully compliant, partially compliant, introducing additional risk, or out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may require a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 33, use the autoscaling signal to verify the assurance level and the load test to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 33 — Large-Scale Scaling, Quotas, and Capacity Planning, objective SAP-C02 2.4 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve the existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./visuals/0/title: Decision Map: Large-Scale Scaling, Quotas, and Capacity Planning
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to the capacity model, service quota, autoscaling signal, and load test in the BlueRetail case.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision Analysis for Large-Scale Scaling, Quotas, and Capacity Planning
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must handle a scenario in which “a sales event exceeds forecasts and a dependency reaches its quota before compute can scale” while still being able to explain the impact on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the capacity model and the responsibility of the service quota.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to the autoscaling signal.
The operations team must observe and reassess the load test using real data.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the capacity model perspective at step 1.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address the service quota by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be verified.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the service quota perspective at step 2.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address the autoscaling signal by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be verified.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the autoscaling signal perspective at step 3.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address the load test by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be verified.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines the capacity model with the service quota, uses the autoscaling signal as a guardrail, and uses the load test as operational evidence.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare the options using the same quality attributes and time horizon.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would trigger reconsideration of the decision.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize the capacity model when the primary requirement lies at step 1 of the decision.

sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize the service quota when the primary requirement lies at step 2 of the decision.

sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./comparison/2/bestFit: Prioritize the autoscaling signal when the primary requirement lies at step 3 of the decision.

sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./commonMistakes/0/better: Evaluate the capacity model against the requirement, constraint, and service quota before choosing.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 33, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.

sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 33, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.

sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/title: Architecture Review: Large-Scale Scaling, Quotas, and Capacity Planning
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because a sales event exceeds forecasts and a dependency reaches its quota before compute can scale. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs cost commitments.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads, translating requirements into decisions and clearly communicating trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are capacity model → service quota → autoscaling signal → load test.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the capacity model.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must show the role of the service quota.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the autoscaling signal without relying on assumptions.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the load test trade-off.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the capacity model, service quota, and autoscaling signal.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraint, and unverified assumptions.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request/data flow with the trust boundary and blast radius.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data direction.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision is traceable to the capacity model and adds no assumptions beyond the question.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents service selection based on preference or a single keyword.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the service quota, autoscaling signal, and load test using the same criteria.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always requires balancing competing factors; there is no universally best option.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the capacity model; the service quota is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing the autoscaling signal is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of the load test without adding value.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the capacity model and service quota and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it suits the scenario in which a sales event exceeds forecasts and a dependency reaches its quota before compute can scale.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the question eliminated the most options?
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./recap/0: Large-scale scaling, quotas, and capacity planning must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./recap/1: The capacity model, service quota, autoscaling signal, and load test form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-33-scaling-quotas-capacity./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.

sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./title: Loosely Coupled Architecture Across Multiple Domains
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./shortTitle: Loosely Coupled Architecture Across Multiple Domains
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./summary: Understand loosely coupled architecture across multiple domains through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be clearly explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./opening/0: BlueRetail is transforming its architecture, and a slowdown in one central service causes multiple products to stop because of synchronous calls and a shared schema. A diagram containing many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team has the capacity to manage it. Lesson 34 starts with this scenario and turns loosely coupled architecture across multiple domains into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one is the best match for keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” Loosely coupled architecture across multiple domains should be evaluated from four perspectives: domain boundary, event contract, queue buffer, and failure isolation. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers when they conflict with a requirement or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 34 — Loosely Coupled Architecture Across Multiple Domains is aligned with objective SAP-C02 2.4 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/0/title: Thinking Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business specifies what must continue operating and what level of loss is acceptable; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. In a loosely coupled architecture across multiple domains, the domain boundary is the entry point, the event contract defines how the system responds, and the queue buffer and failure isolation determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that works for every exercise is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the desired outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to loosely coupled architecture across multiple domains, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but unsuitable for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 34 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the narrative breaks at the domain boundary or event contract, the diagram still contains an unvalidated assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Work Together
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/1/paragraphs/0: domain boundary is the first building block in a loosely coupled architectural decision across multiple domains, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the domain boundary, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, a slowdown in one central service causes multiple products to stop because of synchronous calls and a shared schema. Connecting the domain boundary to the event contract reveals where responsibility lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/1/paragraphs/1: event contract is the second building block in a loosely coupled architectural decision across multiple domains, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the event contract, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, a slowdown in one central service causes multiple products to stop because of synchronous calls and a shared schema. Connecting the event contract to the queue buffer reveals where responsibility lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/1/paragraphs/2: queue buffer is the third building block in a loosely coupled architectural decision across multiple domains, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the queue buffer, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, a slowdown in one central service causes multiple products to stop because of synchronous calls and a shared schema. Connecting the queue buffer to failure isolation reveals where responsibility lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/1/paragraphs/3: failure isolation is the fourth building block in a loosely coupled architectural decision across multiple domains, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by failure isolation, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, a slowdown in one central service causes multiple products to stop because of synchronous calls and a shared schema. Connecting failure isolation to the domain boundary reveals where responsibility lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Record the Trade-offs
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving the domain boundary should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the domain boundary with the queue buffer using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 34, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would require the architecture to be reassessed.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving the event contract should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the event contract with failure isolation using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 34, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would require the architecture to be reassessed.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving the queue buffer should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the queue buffer with the domain boundary using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 34, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would require the architecture to be reassessed.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving failure isolation should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare failure isolation with the event contract using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 34, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would require the architecture to be reassessed.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before It Occurs
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in a loosely coupled architecture across multiple domains occurs when the project team assumes that the domain boundary automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the domain boundary, identify what AWS is responsible for, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in a loosely coupled architecture across multiple domains occurs when the project team assumes that the event contract automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the event contract, identify what AWS is responsible for, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in a loosely coupled architecture across multiple domains occurs when the project team assumes that the queue buffer automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the queue buffer, identify what AWS is responsible for, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has physical stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 34, a slowdown in one central service causes multiple products to stop because of synchronous calls and a shared schema. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and product teams need to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the domain boundary, event contract, and queue buffer. Each option is scored using the same criteria: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. failure isolation serves as the final validation criterion. If the winning option prevails only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for a loosely coupled architecture across multiple domains is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger a reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/5/title: Selecting the Right Action in SAP-C02 Questions
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the mandatory outcome, the constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For loosely coupled architecture across multiple domains, mark every detail related to the domain boundary and event contract. Then classify the answers as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a completely different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 34, use the queue buffer to assess the guarantee level and failure isolation to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the correct feature but the wrong guarantee level is still a distractor.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 34 — Loosely Coupled Architecture Across Multiple Domains, objective SAP-C02 2.4 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. Conclude each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./visuals/0/title: Decision Map: Loosely Coupled Architecture Across Multiple Domains
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to the domain boundary, event contract, queue buffer, and failure isolation in the BlueRetail case.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision Analysis for Loosely Coupled Architecture Across Multiple Domains
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the scenario in which “a slowdown in one central service causes multiple products to stop because of synchronous calls and a shared schema” while still being able to explain the impact on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the domain boundary and the responsibilities of the event contract.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to the queue buffer.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess failure isolation.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the domain boundary perspective in step 1.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address the event contract by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be assessed.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the event contract perspective in step 2.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address the queue buffer by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be assessed.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the queue buffer perspective in step 3.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address failure isolation by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be assessed.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines the domain boundary with the event contract, uses the queue buffer as a guardrail, and uses failure isolation as operational evidence.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare options using the same quality attributes and time horizon.

sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would require the decision to be reassessed.

sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to the domain boundary.

sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/title: Architecture Review: Loosely Coupled Architecture Across Multiple Domains
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because a slowdown in one central service causes multiple products to stop due to synchronous calls and a shared schema. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs cost commitments.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly communicate trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are domain boundary → event contract → queue buffer → failure isolation.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the domain boundary.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the event contract.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations teams must be able to observe the queue buffer without relying on assumptions.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving failure isolation must be accepted by stakeholders.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the domain boundary, event contract, and queue buffer.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flows together with the trust boundary and blast radius.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow directions.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix comparing at least two options.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/deliverables/2: A short architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the domain boundary and does not introduce assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents service selection based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the event contract, queue buffer, and failure isolation using the same criteria.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always requires balance; there is no universally best option.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after implementation.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the domain boundary; the event contract is a constraint that directly affects the selection.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option that prioritizes the queue buffer is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of failure isolation without adding value.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the domain boundary and event contract and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which a slowdown in one central service causes multiple products to stop because of synchronous calls and a shared schema.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and overlooking failure modes.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/commonMistakes/2: Claiming that “AWS manages it automatically” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reassess the decision?
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./recap/0: Loosely coupled architecture across multiple domains must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./recap/1: domain boundary, event contract, queue buffer, and failure isolation form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of the domain boundary and event contract?
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode is related to the queue buffer, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./selfCheck/2: Why can failure isolation change the architectural choice?
sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?
