sap-34-loosely-coupled-enterprise./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to verify coverage — Lesson 34

sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./summary: Understand sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture and order process to update payments, inventory, and shipping, but it cannot use a single database transaction across services. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 35 starts with this scenario and turns sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” Sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions should be considered from four perspectives: saga pattern, compensating action, idempotency key, and eventual consistency. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if they conflict with the requirements or create unacceptable additional responsibilities.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 35—Sagas, Idempotency, and Distributed Transactions—is aligned with SAP-C02 objective 2.4 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic inventory is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why not to choose the other options.”
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/0/title: Mental Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions, the saga pattern is the entry point, compensating action defines how the system responds, and idempotency key and eventual consistency determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. Applying R-C-D-E to sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions shows learners that the same service can be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/0/paragraphs/2: A sign that you understand Lesson 35 is that you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data stored, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the saga pattern or compensating action, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Be Connected
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The saga pattern is the first component in decisions about sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the saga pattern, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the order process updates payments, inventory, and shipping but cannot use a single database transaction across services. Connecting the saga pattern with compensating action reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Compensating action is the second component in decisions about sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the compensating action, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the order process updates payments, inventory, and shipping but cannot use a single database transaction across services. Connecting compensating action with idempotency key reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The idempotency key is the third component in decisions about sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the idempotency key, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the order process updates payments, inventory, and shipping but cannot use a single database transaction across services. Connecting the idempotency key with eventual consistency reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Eventual consistency is the fourth component in decisions about sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by eventual consistency, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the order process updates payments, inventory, and shipping but cannot use a single database transaction across services. Connecting eventual consistency with the saga pattern reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Record the Trade-offs
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving the saga pattern should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the saga pattern with the idempotency key using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 35, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture must be reconsidered.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving compensating action should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare compensating action with eventual consistency using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 35, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture must be reconsidered.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving the idempotency key should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the idempotency key with the saga pattern using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 35, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture must be reconsidered.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving eventual consistency should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare eventual consistency with compensating action using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 35, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture must be reconsidered.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before It Occurs
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions occurs when the project team assumes that the saga pattern automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the saga pattern and identify the parts for which AWS is responsible and the parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions occurs when the project team assumes that compensating action automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of compensating action and identify the parts for which AWS is responsible and the parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions occurs when the project team assumes that the idempotency key automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the idempotency key and identify the parts for which AWS is responsible and the parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 35, the order process updates payments, inventory, and shipping but cannot use a single database transaction across services. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team records three options centered on the saga pattern, compensating action, and idempotency key. Each option is scored using the same matrix: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. Eventual consistency serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations share the same understanding of the decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/5/title: Choosing the Right Action in SAP-C02 Questions
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the mandatory outcome, the constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions, mark every detail related to the saga pattern and compensating action. Then classify each answer as fully compliant, partially compliant, introducing additional risk, or out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful scrutiny. They may require a completely different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “cost priority.” In Lesson 35, use the idempotency key to evaluate the guarantee level and eventual consistency to determine whether the operations team can sustain the solution. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong guarantee level is still a distractor.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 35—Sagas, Idempotency, and Distributed Transactions—SAP-C02 objective 2.4 indicates the required depth. With design, you must balance quality attributes; with determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; with improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./visuals/0/title: Decision Map: Sagas, Idempotency, and Distributed Transactions
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to the saga pattern, compensating action, idempotency key, and eventual consistency in the BlueRetail case.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision Analysis: Sagas, Idempotency, and Distributed Transactions
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must handle a scenario in which “the order process updates payments, inventory, and shipping but cannot use a single database transaction across services,” while still being able to explain the impact on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the saga pattern and the responsibility of compensating action.
The solution must include a failure mode and recovery path related to the idempotency key.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess eventual consistency.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the saga pattern perspective in step 1.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address compensating action by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the compensating action perspective in step 2.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address the idempotency key by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the idempotency key perspective in step 3.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address eventual consistency by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select a solution that combines the saga pattern with compensating action, uses the idempotency key as a guardrail, and uses eventual consistency as operational evidence.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare options using the same quality attributes and time horizon.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would trigger reconsideration of the decision.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize the saga pattern when the primary requirement is at step 1 of the decision.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./comparison/0/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the saga pattern to handle a scenario in which the order process updates payments, inventory, and shipping but cannot use a single database transaction across services.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./comparison/0/tradeOff: Compensating action must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize compensating action when the primary requirement is at step 2 of the decision.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses compensating action to handle a scenario in which the order process updates payments, inventory, and shipping but cannot use a single database transaction across services.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./comparison/1/tradeOff: An idempotency key must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./comparison/2/bestFit: Prioritize the idempotency key when the primary requirement is at step 3 of the decision.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses an idempotency key to handle a scenario in which the order process updates payments, inventory, and shipping but cannot use a single database transaction across services.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./comparison/2/tradeOff: Eventual consistency must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the keyword saga pattern and immediately choosing the answer with the same service or feature name.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./commonMistakes/0/better: Evaluate the saga pattern against the requirement, constraints, and compensating action before choosing.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 35, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the keyword compensating action and immediately choosing the answer with the same service or feature name.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./commonMistakes/1/better: Evaluate compensating action against the requirement, constraints, and idempotency key before choosing.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 35, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the keyword idempotency key and immediately choosing the answer with the same service or feature name.

sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./commonMistakes/2/reason: In Lesson 35, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./examFocus/0: Read the verb in SAP-C02 objective 2.4 to determine the required depth of the answer.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to the saga pattern.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that shift risk from compensating action to the idempotency key without actually addressing it.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./examFocus/3: Choose an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence from eventual consistency.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/title: Architecture Review: Sagas, Idempotency, and Distributed Transactions
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the order process updates payments, inventory, and shipping but cannot use a single database transaction across services. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs a cost commitment.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly explain the trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the points to review are saga pattern → compensating action → idempotency key → eventual consistency.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the saga pattern.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of compensating action.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the idempotency key without relying on assumptions.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to mask a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving eventual consistency must be accepted by stakeholders.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the saga pattern, compensating action, and idempotency key.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Map the request and data flow, including trust boundaries and blast radius.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Record the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/deliverables/2: A brief architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision is traceable to the saga pattern and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares compensating action, idempotency key, and eventual consistency using the same criteria.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; there is no universally best option.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the saga pattern; compensating action is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option that prioritizes the idempotency key is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of eventual consistency without adding value.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the saga pattern and compensating action, with a clearly documented blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which the order process updates payments, inventory, and shipping but cannot use a single database transaction across services.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./recap/0: Sagas, idempotency, and distributed transactions must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./recap/1: The saga pattern, compensating action, idempotency key, and eventual consistency form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./recap/2: Every decision needs a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of the saga pattern and compensating action?
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode relates to the idempotency key, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./selfCheck/2: Why can eventual consistency change the architectural choice?
sap-35-distributed-transaction-patterns./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?

sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./summary: Understand cost design for new solutions through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and the new architecture meets the sla, but cost per order increases with traffic faster than revenue. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it effectively. Lesson 36 begins with this scenario to turn cost design for new solutions into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Cost design for new solutions should be evaluated from four perspectives: cost model, unit economics, purchase commitment, and architecture efficiency. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if they conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 36 — Cost Design for New Solutions is aligned with objective SAP-C02 2.6 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic inventory is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose” but also “why the other options were not chosen.”
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/0/title: Mental Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For cost design for new solutions, the cost model is the entry point, unit economics shows how the system responds, and purchase commitment and architecture efficiency determine long-term operational viability. Moving directly from requirements to service names often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that can be used for any problem is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to cost design for new solutions, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but unsuitable for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/0/paragraphs/2: A sign that you understand Lesson 36 is that you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the cost model or unit economics, the diagram still contains an unvalidated assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Be Connected
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/1/paragraphs/0: cost model is the 1st component of a cost design decision for a new solution, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at the question, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs the cost model receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it handles, and the operational costs it creates. At BlueRetail, the new architecture meets the sla, but cost per order increases with traffic faster than revenue. Connecting cost model with unit economics clarifies where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/1/paragraphs/1: unit economics is the 2nd component of a cost design decision for a new solution, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at the question, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs unit economics receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it handles, and the operational costs it creates. At BlueRetail, the new architecture meets the sla, but cost per order increases with traffic faster than revenue. Connecting unit economics with purchase commitment clarifies where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/1/paragraphs/2: purchase commitment is the 3rd component of a cost design decision for a new solution, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at the question, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs purchase commitment receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it handles, and the operational costs it creates. At BlueRetail, the new architecture meets the sla, but cost per order increases with traffic faster than revenue. Connecting purchase commitment with architecture efficiency clarifies where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/1/paragraphs/3: architecture efficiency is the 4th component of a cost design decision for a new solution, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at the question, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs architecture efficiency receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it handles, and the operational costs it creates. At BlueRetail, the new architecture meets the sla, but cost per order increases with traffic faster than revenue. Connecting architecture efficiency with cost model clarifies where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Document Trade-offs
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving cost model should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare cost model with purchase commitment using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 36, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving unit economics should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare unit economics with architecture efficiency using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 36, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving purchase commitment should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare purchase commitment with cost model using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 36, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving architecture efficiency should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare architecture efficiency with unit economics using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 36, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before Failure Occurs
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in cost design for new solutions occurs when the project team assumes that cost model automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of cost model and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in cost design for new solutions occurs when the project team assumes that unit economics automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of unit economics and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in cost design for new solutions occurs when the project team assumes that purchase commitment automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of purchase commitment and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has retail stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 36, the new architecture meets the SLA, but cost per order increases with traffic faster than revenue. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on cost model, unit economics, and purchase commitment. Each option is scored using the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. architecture efficiency serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for cost design for new solutions is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not satisfy a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For cost design for new solutions, mark every detail related to cost model and unit economics. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” must be examined carefully. They may require a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 36, use purchase commitment to verify the assurance level and architecture efficiency to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the correct feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 36 — Cost Design for New Solutions, objective SAP-C02 2.6 indicates the required depth of knowledge. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the information and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./visuals/0/title: Decision Map: Cost Design for New Solutions
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements with cost model, unit economics, purchase commitment, and architecture efficiency in the BlueRetail case.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Cost Design Decision Analysis for New Solutions
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the scenario in which “the new architecture meets the SLA, but cost per order increases with traffic faster than revenue” while still being able to explain the effects on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of cost model and the responsibility of unit economics.
The option must include a failure mode and a recovery path related to purchase commitment.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess architecture efficiency.

sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 36, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.

sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to cost model.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that shift risk from unit economics to purchase commitment without actually addressing it.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./examFocus/3: Select an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence provided through architecture efficiency.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/title: Architecture Review: Cost Design for New Solutions
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the new architecture meets the sla, but cost per order increases with traffic faster than revenue. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or makes a financial commitment.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads, translating requirements into decisions and clearly communicating trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are cost model → unit economics → purchase commitment → architecture efficiency.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to cost model.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of unit economics.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe purchase commitment without relying on assumptions.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving architecture efficiency must be accepted by stakeholders.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on cost model, unit economics, and purchase commitment.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraint, and unvalidated assumptions.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Map the request/data flow together with the trust boundary and blast radius.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that do not satisfy mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data direction.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix containing at least two options.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to cost model and introduces no assumptions beyond the question.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting services based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares unit economics, purchase commitment, and architecture efficiency using the same criteria.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; there is no universally best option.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and validated after implementation.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around cost model; unit economics is a constraint that directly affects the selection.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option that prioritizes purchase commitment is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of architecture efficiency without adding value.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines cost model and unit economics and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which the new architecture meets the sla, but cost per order increases with traffic faster than revenue.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it automatically” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the question eliminated the most options?
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./recap/0: Cost design for new solutions must begin with the requirement and constraint.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./recap/1: cost model, unit economics, purchase commitment, and architecture efficiency form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that do not satisfy mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-36-new-solution-cost-architecture./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of cost model and unit economics?

sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./title: Choosing databases for an application portfolio
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./shortTitle: Choosing databases for an application portfolio
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./summary: Learn how to choose databases for an application portfolio by evaluating requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architecture decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./opening/0: BlueRetail is transforming its architecture, and many teams default to relational databases even though their access patterns, scale, and audit requirements differ. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team has sufficient capacity to manage it. Lesson 37 starts with this scenario and turns database selection for an application portfolio into an evidence-based decision process rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one is the best fit for keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” Choosing databases for an application portfolio requires considering four perspectives: relational workload, key value workload, graph workload, and ledger requirement. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 37 — Choosing Databases for an Application Portfolio is aligned with objective SAP-C02 2.5 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options were not chosen.”
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss. The application defines traffic, data, and dependencies. The platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. When choosing databases for an application portfolio, the relational workload is the entry point, the key value workload indicates how the system responds, and the graph workload and ledger requirement determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for any problem is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome. A constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills. A decision is the selected architecture structure. Evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. Applying R-C-D-E to choosing databases for an application portfolio shows learners that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but unsuitable for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 37 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross? Which identity is evaluated? Where is the data retained? Which components can fail, and which mechanisms restore them? If the story breaks at the relational workload or key value workload, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/0: relational workload is the 1st component in the decision to choose databases for an application portfolio, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the relational workload, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, many teams default to relational databases even though their access patterns, scale, and audit requirements differ. Connecting the relational workload to the key value workload reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/1: key value workload is the 2nd component in the decision to choose databases for an application portfolio, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the key value workload, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, many teams default to relational databases even though their access patterns, scale, and audit requirements differ. Connecting the key value workload to the graph workload reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/2: graph workload is the 3rd component in the decision to choose databases for an application portfolio, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the graph workload, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, many teams default to relational databases even though their access patterns, scale, and audit requirements differ. Connecting the graph workload to the ledger requirement reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/3: ledger requirement is the 4th component in the decision to choose databases for an application portfolio, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the ledger requirement, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, many teams default to relational databases even though their access patterns, scale, and audit requirements differ. Connecting the ledger requirement to the relational workload reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving a relational workload should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the relational workload with the graph workload using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 37, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a key value workload should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the key value workload with the ledger requirement using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 37, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving a graph workload should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the graph workload with the relational workload using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 37, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving a ledger requirement should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the ledger requirement with the key value workload using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 37, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in choosing databases for an application portfolio occurs when the project team assumes that the relational workload automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the relational workload, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in choosing databases for an application portfolio occurs when the project team assumes that the key value workload automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the key value workload, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in choosing databases for an application portfolio occurs when the project team assumes that the graph workload automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the graph workload, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has retail stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 37, many teams default to relational databases even though their access patterns, scale, and audit requirements differ. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and audit trails, finance needs cost attribution, and product teams need to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the relational workload, key value workload, and graph workload. Each option is scored against the same criteria: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of use. The ledger requirement serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for choosing databases for an application portfolio is written in four sentences: which structure is selected, which requirements it satisfies, which trade-off is accepted, and which metric or review will trigger a reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not satisfy a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/5/title: Reasoning about the required action in SAP-C02 questions
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. When choosing databases for an application portfolio, mark every detail related to the relational workload and key value workload. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service. AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 37, use the graph workload to validate the assurance level and the ledger requirement to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the correct feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 37 — Choosing Databases for an Application Portfolio, objective SAP-C02 2.5 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes. For determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option. For improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architecture decision.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./visuals/0/title: Decision map: Choosing databases for an application portfolio
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting the requirement to the relational workload, key value workload, graph workload, and ledger requirement in the BlueRetail case.

sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the situation in which “many teams default to relational databases even though their access patterns, scale, and audit requirements differ,” while still being able to explain the effects on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the relational workload and the responsibilities of the key value workload.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to the graph workload.
