sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/title: Architecture review: Choosing databases for an application portfolio
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because many teams default to relational databases even though their access patterns, scale, and audit requirements differ. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs a cost commitment.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly communicate trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are relational workload → key value workload → graph workload → ledger requirement.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the relational workload.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the key value workload.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the graph workload without relying on assumptions.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off involving the ledger requirement.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the relational workload, key value workload, and graph workload.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request and data flow, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail to satisfy mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/deliverables/2: A brief architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the relational workload and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the key value workload, graph workload, and ledger requirement using the same criteria.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; there is no universally best option.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, metric, or validation test.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the relational workload; the key value workload is a constraint that directly affects the selection.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option that prioritizes the graph workload is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of the ledger requirement without adding value.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the relational workload and key value workload, clearly documenting the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which many teams default to relational databases even though their access patterns, scale, and audit requirements differ.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirements.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer’s responsibilities.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?

sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./recap/0: Choosing databases for an application portfolio must begin with requirements and constraints.

sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail to satisfy mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.

sap-37-enterprise-database-selection./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to verify coverage — Lesson 37

sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./title: Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./shortTitle: Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./summary: Understand Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architecture decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture: its serverless application creates too many connections, and the business needs global reads and a recovery Region. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 38 starts with this scenario and turns Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy should be evaluated from four perspectives: global database, reader topology, RDS Proxy, and the failover runbook. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 38—Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 2.4 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business specifies what must continue operating and what level of loss is acceptable; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy, the global database is the starting point, the reader topology defines how the system responds, and RDS Proxy plus the failover runbook determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the desired outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architecture; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload and wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 38 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundaries does the request cross, which identities are evaluated, where is the data stored, which components can fail, and which mechanisms provide recovery? If the story breaks at the global database or reader topology, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The global database is the first component in the Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy decision, but its value emerges only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what the service does. Identify the input received by the global database, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, the serverless application creates too many connections, while the business needs global reads and a recovery Region. Connecting the global database to the reader topology reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The reader topology is the second component in the Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy decision, but its value emerges only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what the service does. Identify the input received by the reader topology, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, the serverless application creates too many connections, while the business needs global reads and a recovery Region. Connecting the reader topology to RDS Proxy reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/1/paragraphs/2: RDS Proxy is the third component in the Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy decision, but its value emerges only when considered alongside the business requirements. sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving the global database should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region constraints, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the global database with RDS Proxy using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 38, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving the reader topology should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region constraints, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy arises when the project team assumes that the global database automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the global database, mark what AWS is responsible for, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is not only correct while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy arises when the project team assumes that the reader topology automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the reader topology, mark what AWS is responsible for, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is not only correct while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy arises when the project team assumes that RDS Proxy automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of RDS Proxy, mark what AWS is responsible for, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is not only correct while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 38, the serverless application creates too many connections, while the business needs global reads and a recovery Region. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the global database, reader topology, and RDS Proxy. Each option is scored using the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. The failover runbook serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the team must revisit the constraints.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy is written in four sentences: which architecture is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/5/title: Choosing the right action in SAP-C02 questions
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy, mark every detail related to the global database and reader topology. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it names the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may call for a completely different design than phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 38, use RDS Proxy to evaluate the level of assurance and the failover runbook to determine whether the operations team can sustain the solution. An answer with the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 38—Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy—objective SAP-C02 2.4 indicates the required depth of understanding. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architecture decision.

sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/title: Architecture review: Aurora Global Database and RDS Proxy
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the serverless application creates too many connections, while the business needs global reads and a recovery Region. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs a cost commitment.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly communicate trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are global database → reader topology → RDS Proxy → failover runbook.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the global database.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must show the role of the reader topology.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe RDS Proxy without relying on assumptions.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off associated with the failover runbook.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the global database, reader topology, and RDS Proxy.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Map the request and data flows, including trust boundaries and blast radius.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow directions.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix comparing at least two options.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record that includes trade-offs and evidence.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the global database and does not introduce assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents service selection based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the reader topology, RDS Proxy, and failover runbook using the same criteria.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; no option is universally best.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is reframed around the global database; the reader topology is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing RDS Proxy is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of the failover runbook without adding value.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the global database and reader topology, with a clearly documented blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which the serverless application creates too many connections, while the business needs global reads and a recovery Region.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-38-aurora-global-rds-proxy./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.

sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./shortTitle: DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./summary: Understand DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and active-active traffic is creating hot keys and concurrent updates to the same item in multiple Regions. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 39 starts with this scenario and turns DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design must be considered from four perspectives: global table, partition distribution, write conflict, and adaptive capacity. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 39—DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 2.4 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to check coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions have been validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable. The application team describes traffic, data, and dependencies. The platform team translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design, the global table is the entry point, partition distribution shows how the system responds, and write conflict and adaptive capacity determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that works for any lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the desired outcome. A constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills. A decision is the selected architectural structure. Evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload and wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 39 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross? Which identity is evaluated? Where is the data retained? Which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story stops at the global table or partition distribution, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/1/paragraphs/0: global table is the first component in decisions about DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by the global table, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, active-active traffic creates hot keys and concurrent updates to the same item in multiple Regions. Connecting the global table to partition distribution reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/1/paragraphs/1: partition distribution is the second component in decisions about DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by partition distribution, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, active-active traffic creates hot keys and concurrent updates to the same item in multiple Regions. Connecting partition distribution to write conflict reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.

For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by write conflict, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, active-active traffic creates hot keys and concurrent updates to the same item in multiple Regions. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by adaptive capacity, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving a global table should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quotas, Region limitations, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the global table with write conflict using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and explain how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 39, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving partition distribution should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quotas, Region limitations, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. Next, compare partition distribution with adaptive capacity using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and explain how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 39, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving write conflict should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quotas, Region limitations, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. Next, compare write conflict with the global table using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and explain how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 39, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving adaptive capacity should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quotas, Region limitations, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. Next, compare adaptive capacity with partition distribution using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and explain how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 39, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/3/title: Design for failures before they occur
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design occurs when the project team assumes that the global table automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the global table and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design occurs when the project team assumes that partition distribution automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of partition distribution and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design occurs when the project team assumes that write conflict automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of write conflict and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 39, active-active traffic creates hot keys and concurrent updates to the same item in multiple Regions. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a consistent experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder will create operational debt for another.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the global table, partition distribution, and write conflict. Each option is scored against the same criteria: mandatory requirement coverage, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of consumption. adaptive capacity serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design is written in four sentences: which structure is selected, which requirements it satisfies, which trade-off is accepted, and which metric or review will trigger a reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design, mark every detail related to the global table and partition distribution. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service. AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful analysis. They may require a fundamentally different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 39, use write conflict to evaluate the required assurance level and adaptive capacity to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 39—DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design—objective SAP-C02 2.4 indicates the required depth of knowledge. For design, you must balance quality attributes. For determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option. For improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision analysis for DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the scenario in which “active-active traffic creates hot keys and concurrent updates to the same item in multiple Regions” while still being able to explain the impact on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the global table and the responsibilities of partition distribution.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to write conflict.
The operations team must observe and reassess adaptive capacity using real data.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the global table perspective in step 1.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address partition distribution by itself, so the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the partition distribution perspective in step 2.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address write conflict by itself, so the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the write conflict perspective in step 3.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address adaptive capacity by itself, so the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines the global table with partition distribution, uses write conflict as a guardrail, and uses adaptive capacity as operational evidence.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that do not satisfy mandatory requirements before comparing costs.

sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize partition distribution when the primary requirement is addressed in step 2 of the decision.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses partition distribution to address the scenario in which active-active traffic creates hot keys and concurrent updates to the same item in multiple Regions.

sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses write conflict to address the scenario in which active-active traffic creates hot keys and concurrent updates to the same item in multiple Regions.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./comparison/2/tradeOff: Adaptive capacity must be added or evaluated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.

sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/title: Architecture review: DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because active-active traffic is creating hot keys and concurrent updates to the same item in multiple Regions. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs cost commitments.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly explain the trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are global table → partition distribution → write conflict → adaptive capacity.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the global table.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must show the role of partition distribution.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations teams must be able to observe write conflict without relying on assumptions.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving adaptive capacity must be accepted by stakeholders.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the global table, partition distribution, and write conflict.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request and data flows together with the trust boundaries and blast radius.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that do not satisfy mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the global table and adds no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares partition distribution, write conflict, and adaptive capacity using the same criteria.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; no option is absolutely best.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and validated after implementation.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the global table; partition distribution is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option prioritizing write conflict is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of adaptive capacity without adding value.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the global table with partition distribution and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which active-active traffic creates hot keys and concurrent updates to the same item in multiple Regions.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying that “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./recap/0: DynamoDB global tables and advanced partition design must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./recap/1: global table, partition distribution, write conflict, and adaptive capacity form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-39-dynamodb-global-design./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that do not satisfy mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
