sap-42-serverless-enterprise./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize the serverless boundary when the primary requirement is associated with step 1 of the decision.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./comparison/0/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the serverless boundary to address the situation in which hundreds of functions are growing rapidly, but quotas, dependencies, and event ownership are not yet governed.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./comparison/0/tradeOff: Concurrency governance must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize concurrency governance when the primary requirement is associated with step 2 of the decision.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses concurrency governance to address the situation in which hundreds of functions are growing rapidly, but quotas, dependencies, and event ownership are not yet governed.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./comparison/1/tradeOff: The event source must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./comparison/2/bestFit: Prioritize the event source when the primary requirement is associated with step 3 of the decision.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the event source to address the situation in which hundreds of functions are growing rapidly, but quotas, dependencies, and event ownership are not yet governed.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./comparison/2/tradeOff: The service quota must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the keyword serverless boundary and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./commonMistakes/0/better: Evaluate the serverless boundary against the requirement, constraint, and concurrency governance before choosing.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 42, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the keyword concurrency governance and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.

sap-42-serverless-enterprise./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 42, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.

sap-42-serverless-enterprise./commonMistakes/2/reason: In Lesson 42, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAP-C02 2.4 to determine the required response depth.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to the serverless boundary.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that shift risk from concurrency governance to the event source without actually addressing it.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./examFocus/3: Choose an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence from the service quota.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/title: Architecture review: Serverless at enterprise scale
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because hundreds of functions are growing rapidly, but quotas, dependencies, and event ownership are not yet governed. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or makes cost commitments.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads, translating requirements into decisions and clearly communicating trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are serverless boundary → concurrency governance → event source → service quota.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the serverless boundary.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of concurrency governance.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the event source without relying on assumptions.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off related to the service quota.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as an evaluable criterion.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the serverless boundary, concurrency governance, and event source.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request and data flows together with the trust boundary and blast radius.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/deliverables/2: A brief architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision is traceable to the serverless boundary and adds no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting a service based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares concurrency governance, the event source, and the service quota using the same criteria.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing trade-offs; there is no universally best option.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the serverless boundary; concurrency governance is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing the event source is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of the service quota without adding value.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the serverless boundary and concurrency governance and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which hundreds of functions are growing rapidly, but quotas, dependencies, and event ownership are not yet governed.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and overlooking failure modes.
sap-42-serverless-enterprise./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.

sap-43-api-event-integration./title: APIs, Event Buses, and Cross-Organizational Integration
sap-43-api-event-integration./shortTitle: APIs, Event Buses, and Cross-Organizational Integration
sap-43-api-event-integration./summary: Understand APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-43-api-event-integration./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and its subsidiaries need to exchange events without creating point-to-point connections or sharing credentials. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 43 starts with this scenario and turns APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-43-api-event-integration./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration should be evaluated from four perspectives: API contract, event bus policy, schema registry, and cross-account integration. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-43-api-event-integration./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 43—APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 2.4 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/0/title: Mental Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application team describes traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform team translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration, the API contract is the entry point, the event bus policy defines how the system responds, and the schema registry and cross-account integration determine long-term operability. Moving directly from requirements to service names often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that applies to every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but unsuitable for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 43 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the API contract or event bus policy, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Work Together
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The API contract is the first component in decisions about APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input accepted by the API contract, the state or policy it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, subsidiaries need to exchange events without creating point-to-point connections or sharing credentials. Connecting the API contract to the event bus policy reveals where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The event bus policy is the second component in decisions about APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input accepted by the event bus policy, the state or policy it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, subsidiaries need to exchange events without creating point-to-point connections or sharing credentials. Connecting the event bus policy to the schema registry reveals where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The schema registry is the third component in decisions about APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input accepted by the schema registry, the state or policy it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, subsidiaries need to exchange events without creating point-to-point connections or sharing credentials. Connecting the schema registry to cross-account integration reveals where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Cross-account integration is the fourth component in decisions about APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input accepted by cross-account integration, the state or policy it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, subsidiaries need to exchange events without creating point-to-point connections or sharing credentials. Connecting cross-account integration to the API contract reveals where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Record the Trade-offs
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving an API contract should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the API contract with the schema registry using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 43, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural reassessment.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving an event bus policy should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the event bus policy with cross-account integration using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 43, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural reassessment.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving a schema registry should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the schema registry with the API contract using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 43, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural reassessment.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving cross-account integration should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare cross-account integration with the event bus policy using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 43, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural reassessment.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before It Occurs
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration occurs when the project team assumes that the API contract automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the API contract's blast radius, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also includes a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration occurs when the project team assumes that the event bus policy automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the event bus policy's blast radius, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also includes a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration occurs when the project team assumes that the schema registry automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the schema registry's blast radius, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also includes a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 43, subsidiaries need to exchange events without creating point-to-point connections or sharing credentials. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and product teams need to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the API contract, event bus policy, and schema registry. Each option is scored using the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of use. Cross-account integration serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration is written in four sentences: which structure is selected, which requirements it satisfies, which trade-off is accepted, and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many appealing features.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/5/title: How to Select an Action in SAP-C02 Questions
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration, mark every detail related to the API contract and event bus policy. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 43, use the schema registry to verify the assurance level and cross-account integration to determine whether the operations team can maintain the option. An answer that has the right feature but provides the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-43-api-event-integration./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 43—APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration—objective SAP-C02 2.4 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-43-api-event-integration./visuals/0/title: Decision Map: APIs, Event Buses, and Cross-Organizational Integration
sap-43-api-event-integration./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to the API contract, event bus policy, schema registry, and cross-account integration in the BlueRetail case.
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision Analysis for APIs, Event Buses, and Cross-Organizational Integration
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the scenario in which “subsidiaries need to exchange events without creating point-to-point connections or sharing credentials,” while still being able to explain the impact on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the API contract and the responsibility of the event bus policy.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to the schema registry.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess cross-account integration.
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the API contract perspective in step 1.
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address the event bus policy by itself, so the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the event bus policy perspective in step 2.
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address the schema registry by itself, so the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the schema registry perspective in step 3.
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address cross-account integration by itself, so the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines the API contract with the event bus policy, uses the schema registry as a guardrail, and uses cross-account integration as operational evidence.
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare options using the same quality attributes and time horizon.
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Explicitly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
sap-43-api-event-integration./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Define the metric or review that would trigger reassessment of the decision.
sap-43-api-event-integration./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize the API contract when the primary requirement is associated with step 1 of the decision.

sap-43-api-event-integration./comparison/0/tradeOff: The event bus policy must be added or reviewed; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-43-api-event-integration./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize the event bus policy when the primary requirement is associated with step 2 of the decision.
sap-43-api-event-integration./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the event bus policy to address the need for subsidiaries to exchange events without creating point-to-point connections or sharing credentials.
sap-43-api-event-integration./comparison/1/tradeOff: The schema registry must be added or reviewed; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-43-api-event-integration./comparison/2/bestFit: Prioritize the schema registry when the primary requirement is associated with step 3 of the decision.
sap-43-api-event-integration./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the schema registry to address the need for subsidiaries to exchange events without creating point-to-point connections or sharing credentials.
sap-43-api-event-integration./comparison/2/tradeOff: Cross-account integration must be added or reviewed; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-43-api-event-integration./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the keyword API contract and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.

sap-43-api-event-integration./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 43, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.

sap-43-api-event-integration./commonMistakes/1/better: Evaluate the event bus policy against the requirements, constraints, and schema registry before choosing.
sap-43-api-event-integration./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 43, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
sap-43-api-event-integration./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the keyword schema registry and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.
sap-43-api-event-integration./commonMistakes/2/better: Evaluate the schema registry against the requirements, constraints, and cross-account integration before choosing.
sap-43-api-event-integration./commonMistakes/2/reason: In Lesson 43, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
sap-43-api-event-integration./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAP-C02 2.4 to determine the required depth of the answer.
sap-43-api-event-integration./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to the API contract.
sap-43-api-event-integration./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that shift risk from the event bus policy to the schema registry without actually addressing it.
sap-43-api-event-integration./examFocus/3: Select an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence from cross-account integration.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/title: Architecture Review: APIs, Event Buses, and Cross-Organizational Integration
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because its subsidiaries need to exchange events without creating point-to-point connections or sharing credentials. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs a cost commitment.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly explain the trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are API contract → event bus policy → schema registry → cross-account integration.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the API contract.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the event bus policy.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations teams must be able to observe the schema registry without relying on assumptions.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off associated with cross-account integration.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the API contract, event bus policy, and schema registry.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request and data flow with trust boundaries and blast radius.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the API contract and adds no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting services based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the event bus policy, schema registry, and cross-account integration using the same criteria.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; no option is universally best.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after implementation.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the API contract; the event bus policy is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing the schema registry is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of cross-account integration without adding value.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the API contract and event bus policy and explicitly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the need for subsidiaries to exchange events without creating point-to-point connections or sharing credentials.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/commonMistakes/2: Claiming that “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-43-api-event-integration./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-43-api-event-integration./recap/0: APIs, event buses, and cross-organizational integration must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-43-api-event-integration./recap/1: The API contract, event bus policy, schema registry, and cross-account integration form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-43-api-event-integration./recap/2: Every decision needs a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-43-api-event-integration./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-43-api-event-integration./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of the API contract and the event bus policy?
sap-43-api-event-integration./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode is associated with the schema registry, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
sap-43-api-event-integration./selfCheck/2: Why can cross-account integration change the architectural choice?
sap-43-api-event-integration./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?

sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./title: Selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./shortTitle: Selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./summary: Learn how to select analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes by evaluating requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and the business wants AI for every product, but the data, latency requirements, and level of human oversight vary significantly. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it effectively. Lesson 44 begins with this scenario and turns outcome-based analytics, ML, and AI service selection into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes requires considering four perspectives: business outcome, managed AI service, data readiness, and operational risk. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if they conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 44—Selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 2.5 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to select,” but also “why the other options should not be selected.”
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable. The application describes traffic, data, and dependencies. The platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. When selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes, the business outcome is the entry point, the managed AI service determines how the system responds, and data readiness and operational risk determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every scenario is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A Requirement describes the outcome. A Constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills. A Decision is the selected architectural structure. Evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes, learners can see how the same service may be correct for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 44 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross? Which identity is evaluated? Where is the data retained? Which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the business outcome or managed AI service, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/0: business outcome is the first element in selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the business outcome, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the business wants AI for every product, but the data, latency requirements, and level of human oversight vary significantly. Connecting the business outcome to the managed AI service reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different results. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/1: managed AI service is the second element in selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the managed AI service, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the business wants AI for every product, but the data, latency requirements, and level of human oversight vary significantly. Connecting the managed AI service to data readiness reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different results. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/2: data readiness is the third element in selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by data readiness, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the business wants AI for every product, but the data, latency requirements, and level of human oversight vary significantly. Connecting data readiness to operational risk reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different results. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/1/paragraphs/3: operational risk is the fourth element in selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by operational risk, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, the business wants AI for every product, but the data, latency requirements, and level of human oversight vary significantly. Connecting operational risk to the business outcome reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different results. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving the business outcome should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the business outcome with data readiness using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 44, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a managed AI service should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the managed AI service with operational risk using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 44, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving data readiness should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare data readiness with the business outcome using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 44, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving operational risk should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare operational risk with the managed AI service using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 44, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/3/title: Design for failures before they occur
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes occurs when the project team assumes that the business outcome automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the business outcome and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that an answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes occurs when the project team assumes that the managed AI service automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the managed AI service and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that an answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes occurs when the project team assumes that data readiness automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of data readiness and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that an answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 44, the business wants AI for every product, but the data, latency requirements, and level of human oversight vary significantly. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and product teams need to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the business outcome, managed AI service, and data readiness. Each option is evaluated against the same scorecard: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of consumption. operational risk serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the team must revisit the constraints.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes is written in four sentences: which structure is selected, which requirements it satisfies, which trade-off is accepted, and which metric or review will trigger a reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not meet a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/5/title: Selecting the right action in SAP-C02 questions
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. When selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes, mark every detail related to the business outcome and managed AI service. Then classify each answer as fully compliant, partially compliant, introducing additional risk, or out of scope. Do not select an answer merely because it mentions the correct service. AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may require a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 44, use data readiness to verify the required assurance level and operational risk to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the correct feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 44—Selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes—objective SAP-C02 2.5 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes. For determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option. For improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./visuals/0/title: Decision map: Selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting the requirement to the business outcome, managed AI service, data readiness, and operational risk in the BlueRetail case.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision analysis: Selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the scenario in which “the business wants AI for every product, but the data, latency requirements, and level of human oversight vary significantly,” while still being able to explain the effects on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the business outcome and the responsibility of the managed AI service.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to data readiness.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess operational risk.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the business outcome perspective in step 1.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address the managed AI service by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the managed AI service perspective in step 2.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address data readiness by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the data readiness perspective in step 3.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address operational risk by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines the business outcome with a managed AI service, uses data readiness as a guardrail, and uses operational risk as operational evidence.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare options using the same quality attributes and time horizon.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would trigger a reassessment of the decision.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize the business outcome when the primary requirement is in step 1 of the decision.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./comparison/0/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the business outcome to address a scenario in which the business wants AI for every product, but the data, latency requirements, and level of human oversight vary significantly.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./comparison/0/tradeOff: The managed AI service must be added or evaluated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize the managed AI service when the primary requirement is in step 2 of the decision.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses a managed AI service to address a scenario in which the business wants AI for every product, but the data, latency requirements, and level of human oversight vary significantly.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./comparison/1/tradeOff: Data readiness must be added or evaluated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./comparison/2/bestFit: Prioritize data readiness when the primary requirement is in step 3 of the decision.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses data readiness to address a scenario in which the business wants AI for every product, but the data, latency requirements, and level of human oversight vary significantly.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./comparison/2/tradeOff: Operational risk must be added or evaluated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the keyword business outcome and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./commonMistakes/0/better: Evaluate the business outcome against the requirement, constraint, and managed AI service before making a selection.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 44, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.

sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAP-C02 2.5 to determine the required depth of the answer.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to the business outcome.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that shift risk from the managed AI service to data readiness without actually addressing it.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./examFocus/3: Select an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence based on operational risk.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/title: Architecture review: Selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the business wants AI for every product, but the data, latency requirements, and level of human oversight vary significantly. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs a cost commitment.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly communicate trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are business outcome → managed AI service → data readiness → operational risk.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the business outcome.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the managed AI service.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe data readiness without relying on assumptions.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to mask an insufficiently defined requirement.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off involving operational risk.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the business outcome, managed AI service, and data readiness.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flow, including trust boundaries and blast radius.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data direction.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the business outcome and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the managed AI service, data readiness, and operational risk using the same criteria.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing competing factors; there is no universally best option.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The response identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the business outcome; the managed AI service is a constraint that directly affects the selection.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option that prioritizes data readiness is eliminated if it increases the operational burden associated with operational risk without adding value.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the business outcome and managed AI service, and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which the business wants AI for every product, but the data, latency requirements, and level of human oversight vary significantly.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reassess the decision?
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./recap/0: Selecting analytics, ML, and AI services based on outcomes must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./recap/1: business outcome, managed AI service, data readiness, and operational risk form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of the business outcome and managed AI service?
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode relates to data readiness, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./selfCheck/2: Why can operational risk change the architectural choice?
sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?
