sap-44-data-ml-ai-service-selection./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to verify coverage—Lesson 44

sap-45-observability-operating-model./summary: Understand observability based on business outcomes through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an explainable architecture decision for the BlueRetail team.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and the team has thousands of resource alarms but cannot tell which incidents are affecting orders and customers. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 45 begins with this scenario and turns observability based on business outcomes into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Observability based on business outcomes should be evaluated from four perspectives: service level indicator, business KPI, metric log trace, and alert ownership. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 45 — Observability Based on Business Outcomes is aligned with objective SAP-C02 3.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic inventory is used only to check coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose” but also “why not to choose the other options.”
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/0/title: Mental Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business states what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For observability based on business outcomes, the service level indicator is the entry point, the business KPI shows how the system responds, and metric log trace and alert ownership determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that can be used for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architecture structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to observability based on business outcomes, learners can see how the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill matters more than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 45 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the service level indicator or business KPI, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Be Connected
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/1/paragraphs/0: service level indicator is the first component of an observability decision based on business outcomes, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the service level indicator, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the team has thousands of resource alarms but cannot tell which incidents are affecting orders and customers. Connecting the service level indicator to the business KPI reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/1/paragraphs/1: business KPI is the second component of an observability decision based on business outcomes, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the business KPI, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the team has thousands of resource alarms but cannot tell which incidents are affecting orders and customers. Connecting the business KPI to metric log trace reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/1/paragraphs/2: metric log trace is the third component of an observability decision based on business outcomes, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by metric log trace, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the team has thousands of resource alarms but cannot tell which incidents are affecting orders and customers. Connecting metric log trace to alert ownership reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/1/paragraphs/3: alert ownership is the fourth component of an observability decision based on business outcomes, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside the business requirement. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “what is this service used for?” Identify the input received by alert ownership, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the team has thousands of resource alarms but cannot tell which incidents are affecting orders and customers. Connecting alert ownership to the service level indicator reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Record the Trade-offs
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving a service level indicator should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the service level indicator with metric log trace using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total price of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 45, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signal would trigger an architecture review.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a business KPI should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the business KPI with alert ownership using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total price of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 45, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signal would trigger an architecture review.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving metric log trace should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare metric log trace with the service level indicator using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total price of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 45, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signal would trigger an architecture review.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving alert ownership should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare alert ownership with the business KPI using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total price of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 45, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signal would trigger an architecture review.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before It Occurs
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for observability based on business outcomes occurs when the project team assumes that the service level indicator addresses every related layer by itself. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the service level indicator, mark the portion for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is not only correct while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for observability based on business outcomes occurs when the project team assumes that the business KPI addresses every related layer by itself. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the business KPI, mark the portion for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is not only correct while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for observability based on business outcomes occurs when the project team assumes that metric log trace addresses every related layer by itself. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of metric log trace, mark the portion for which AWS is responsible, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is not only correct while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 45, the team has thousands of resource alarms but cannot tell which incidents are affecting orders and customers. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the service level indicator, business KPI, and metric log trace. Each option is evaluated using the same scorecard: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. alert ownership serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for observability based on business outcomes is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger a reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/5/title: How to Select an Action in SAP-C02 Questions
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For observability based on business outcomes, mark every detail related to the service level indicator and business KPI. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not select an answer merely because it mentions the right service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 45, use metric log trace to verify the assurance level and alert ownership to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 45 — Observability Based on Business Outcomes, objective SAP-C02 3.1 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the evidence and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architecture decision.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./visuals/0/title: Decision Map: Observability Based on Business Outcomes
sap-45-observability-operating-model./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to service level indicator, business KPI, metric log trace, and alert ownership in the BlueRetail case.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Observability Decision Analysis Based on Business Outcomes
sap-45-observability-operating-model./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the situation in which “the team has thousands of resource alarms but cannot tell which incidents are affecting orders and customers,” while still being able to explain the effects on security, resiliency, performance, and cost.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the service level indicator and the responsibility of the business KPI.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to metric log trace.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess alert ownership.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the service level indicator perspective in step 1.

sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/title: Architecture Review: Observability Based on Business Outcomes
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the team has thousands of resource alarms but cannot tell which incidents are affecting orders and customers. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs cost commitments.

sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are service level indicator → business KPI → metric log trace → alert ownership.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the service level indicator.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the business KPI.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe metric log trace without relying on assumptions.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to mask a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving alert ownership must be accepted by the stakeholders.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the service level indicator, business KPI, and metric log trace.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Map the request and data flow, including trust boundaries and blast radius.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Record the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and metric review.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix comparing at least two options.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the service level indicator and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting services based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the business KPI, metric log trace, and alert ownership using the same criteria.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing factors; no option is universally best.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the service level indicator; the business KPI is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing metric log trace is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of alert ownership without adding value.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the service level indicator and business KPI and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and metric review.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which the team has thousands of resource alarms but cannot tell which incidents are affecting orders and customers.

sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-45-observability-operating-model./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.

sap-45-observability-operating-model./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to verify coverage — Lesson 45

sap-46-organization-operations-data./title: CloudTrail, Config, and Centralized Operations Data
sap-46-organization-operations-data./shortTitle: CloudTrail, Config, and Centralized Operations Data
sap-46-organization-operations-data./summary: Understand CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and cross-account incident investigations take hours because logs and configuration history are scattered. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 46 starts with this scenario and turns CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data into an evidence-based decision sequence rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data should be considered from four perspectives: organization trail, Config aggregator, operations data lake, and query evidence. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 46—CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 3.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic inventory is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/0/title: Mental Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must continue operating and what level of loss is acceptable. The application defines traffic, data, and dependencies. The platform translates these requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data, the organization trail is the entry point, the Config aggregator shows how the system responds, and the operations data lake and query evidence determine long-term operability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the desired outcome. A constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills. A decision is the selected architectural structure. Evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload and wrong for another. This skill matters more than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 46 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross? Which identity is evaluated? Where is the data retained? Which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the organization trail or Config aggregator, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Work Together
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The organization trail is the first component in a decision about CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what the service does. Identify the input received by the organization trail, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, cross-account incident investigations take hours because logs and configuration history are scattered. Connecting the organization trail to the Config aggregator reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The Config aggregator is the second component in a decision about CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what the service does. Identify the input received by the Config aggregator, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, cross-account incident investigations take hours because logs and configuration history are scattered. Connecting the Config aggregator to the operations data lake reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The operations data lake is the third component in a decision about CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what the service does. Identify the input received by the operations data lake, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, cross-account incident investigations take hours because logs and configuration history are scattered. Connecting the operations data lake to query evidence reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Query evidence is the fourth component in a decision about CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what the service does. Identify the input received by query evidence, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, cross-account incident investigations take hours because logs and configuration history are scattered. Connecting query evidence to the organization trail reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Record the Trade-offs
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving an organization trail should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the organization trail with the operations data lake using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 46, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a Config aggregator should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the Config aggregator with query evidence using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 46, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving an operations data lake should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the operations data lake with the organization trail using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 46, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving query evidence should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare query evidence with the Config aggregator using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 46, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before It Happens
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data occurs when the project team assumes that the organization trail automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the organization trail and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data occurs when the project team assumes that the Config aggregator automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the Config aggregator and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data occurs when the project team assumes that the operations data lake automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the operations data lake and mark what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 46, cross-account incident investigations take hours because logs and configuration history are scattered. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and product teams need to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team records three options centered on the organization trail, Config aggregator, and operations data lake. Each option is scored against the same criteria: mandatory requirement coverage, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. Query evidence serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data is written in four sentences: which structure is selected, which requirements it satisfies, which trade-offs are accepted, and which metric or review will trigger a reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not satisfy a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/5/title: Choosing the Right Action in SAP-C02 Questions
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the final verb in the question. For CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data, mark every detail related to the organization trail and Config aggregator. Then classify the answers as fully meeting the requirements, partially meeting them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service. AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the best-fit solution.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require close examination. They may require a substantially different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “cost prioritized.” In Lesson 46, use the operations data lake to assess the level of assurance and query evidence to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the correct feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 46—CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data—objective SAP-C02 3.1 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes. For determine, you must interpret the evidence and select the cause or option. For improve, you must preserve existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./visuals/0/title: Decision Map: CloudTrail, Config, and Centralized Operations Data
sap-46-organization-operations-data./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to the organization trail, Config aggregator, operations data lake, and query evidence in the BlueRetail case.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision Analysis for CloudTrail, Config, and Centralized Operations Data
sap-46-organization-operations-data./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the situation in which “cross-account incident investigations take hours because logs and configuration history are scattered” while still being able to explain the effects on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the organization trail and the responsibility of the Config aggregator.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to the operations data lake.
The operations team must observe and reassess query evidence using actual data.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the organization trail perspective in step 1.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: It does not address the Config aggregator by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the Config aggregator perspective in step 2.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: It does not address the operations data lake by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: A good fit when the requirement prioritizes the operations data lake perspective in step 3.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: It does not address query evidence by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be evaluated.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines the organization trail with the Config aggregator, uses the operations data lake as a guardrail, and uses query evidence as operational evidence.

sap-46-organization-operations-data./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare options using the same quality attributes and time horizon.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly record the trade-offs, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would trigger a reassessment of the decision.

sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/title: Architecture Review: CloudTrail, Config, and Centralized Operations Data
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because cross-account incident investigations take hours due to scattered logs and configuration history. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs cost commitments.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. Your role is to translate requirements into decisions and clearly communicate trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are organization trail → Config aggregator → operations data lake → query evidence.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the organization trail.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must show the role of the Config aggregator.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations teams must be able to observe the operations data lake without relying on assumptions.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to obscure a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off involving query evidence.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the organization trail, Config aggregator, and operations data lake.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, record the trade-offs, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flows with their trust boundaries and blast radii.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Record the decision, trade-offs, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow directions.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the organization trail and does not introduce assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting services based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the Config aggregator, operations data lake, and query evidence using the same criteria.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; no option is universally best.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validation metric or test.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the organization trail; the Config aggregator is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option that prioritizes the operations data lake is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of query evidence without adding value.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the organization trail and Config aggregator, with a clearly documented blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which cross-account incident investigations take hours because logs and configuration history are scattered.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/commonMistakes/2: Stating that “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-46-organization-operations-data./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-46-organization-operations-data./recap/0: CloudTrail, Config, and centralized operations data must start with requirements and constraints.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./recap/1: The organization trail, Config aggregator, operations data lake, and query evidence form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./recap/2: Every decision needs trade-offs, a recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-46-organization-operations-data./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.

sap-47-systems-manager-automation./title: Systems Manager, Runbooks, and Automated Remediation
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./shortTitle: Systems Manager, Runbooks, and Automated Remediation
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./summary: Understand Systems Manager, runbooks, and automated remediation through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architecture decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and operations repeatedly performs manual fixes, but the automation lacks safety conditions and rollback. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it effectively. Lesson 47 starts with this scenario and turns Systems Manager, runbooks, and automated remediation into an evidence-based decision sequence rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” Systems Manager, runbooks, and automated remediation should be evaluated from four perspectives: automation document, runbook, change control, and remediation guardrail. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if they conflict with the requirements or create unacceptable additional responsibilities.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 47—Systems Manager, runbooks, and automated remediation—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 3.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic inventory is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/0/title: Mental Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For Systems Manager, runbooks, and automated remediation, the automation document is the entry point, the runbook defines how the system responds, and change control and the remediation guardrail determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architecture; and evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to Systems Manager, runbooks, and automated remediation, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You understand Lesson 47 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and what mechanism restores operation? If the story breaks at the automation document or runbook, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Work Together
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The automation document is the first building block in a Systems Manager, runbook, and automated remediation decision, but its value becomes clear only in the context of business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input the automation document receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, operations repeatedly performs manual fixes, but the automation lacks safety conditions and rollback. Connecting the automation document to the runbook reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The runbook is the second building block in a Systems Manager, runbook, and automated remediation decision, but its value becomes clear only in the context of business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input the runbook receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, operations repeatedly performs manual fixes, but the automation lacks safety conditions and rollback. Connecting the runbook to change control reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/1/paragraphs/2: Change control is the third building block in a Systems Manager, runbook, and automated remediation decision, but its value becomes clear only in the context of business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input change control receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, operations repeatedly performs manual fixes, but the automation lacks safety conditions and rollback. Connecting change control to the remediation guardrail reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/1/paragraphs/3: The remediation guardrail is the fourth building block in a Systems Manager, runbook, and automated remediation decision, but its value becomes clear only in the context of business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at “What is this service used for?” Identify the input the remediation guardrail receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, operations repeatedly performs manual fixes, but the automation lacks safety conditions and rollback. Connecting the remediation guardrail to the automation document reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team’s capabilities.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Record the Trade-offs
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving an automation document should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the automation document with change control using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 47, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture reassessment.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a runbook should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the runbook with the remediation guardrail using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 47, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture reassessment.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving change control should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare change control with the automation document using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 47, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture reassessment.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving a remediation guardrail should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the remediation guardrail with the runbook using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 47, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture reassessment.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before It Occurs
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for Systems Manager, runbooks, and automated remediation occurs when the project team assumes that the automation document automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the automation document and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is not only valid while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for Systems Manager, runbooks, and automated remediation occurs when the project team assumes that the runbook automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the runbook and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is not only valid while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for Systems Manager, runbooks, and automated remediation occurs when the project team assumes that change control automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of change control and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is not only valid while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 47, operations repeatedly performs manual fixes, but the automation lacks safety conditions and rollback. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the automation document, runbook, and change control. Each option is scored using the same matrix: compliance with mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. The remediation guardrail serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for Systems Manager, runbooks, and automated remediation is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format gives developers, security, and operations a shared understanding of the decision. In exam questions, the same four sentences help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/5/title: How to Select an Action in SAP-C02 Questions
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For Systems Manager, runbooks, and automated remediation, mark every detail related to the automation document and runbook. Then classify each answer as fully compliant, partially compliant, introducing additional risk, or out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the best-fit solution.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a substantially different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 47, use change control to evaluate the required level of assurance and the remediation guardrail to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 47—Systems Manager, runbooks, and automated remediation—objective SAP-C02 3.1 indicates the required depth of understanding. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architecture decision.

sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/title: Architecture Review: Systems Manager, Runbooks, and Automated Remediation
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because operations repeatedly performs manual fixes, but the automation lacks safety conditions and rollback. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs cost commitments.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads, translating requirements into decisions and clearly communicating trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are automation document → runbook → change control → remediation guardrail.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the automation document.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the runbook.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe change control without relying on assumptions.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving the remediation guardrail must be accepted by stakeholders.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the automation document, runbook, and change control.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraint, and unvalidated assumptions.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request and data flows, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow directions.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the automation document and introduces no assumptions beyond the question.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting services based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the runbook, change control, and remediation guardrail using the same criteria.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always requires balance; there is no universally best option.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the automation document; the runbook is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option that prioritizes change control is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of the remediation guardrail without providing additional value.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the automation document and runbook, with the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric clearly documented.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which operations repeatedly performs manual fixes, but the automation lacks safety conditions and rollback.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it automatically” without identifying the customer’s responsibilities.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the question eliminated the most options?
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reassess the decision?
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./recap/0: Systems Manager, runbooks, and automated remediation must start with requirements and constraints.

sap-47-systems-manager-automation./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-47-systems-manager-automation./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
