sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./title: Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./shortTitle: Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./summary: Understand Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be clearly explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, which has been declared resilient, but the recovery path has never been tested during an actual dependency failure. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 48 begins with this scenario and turns Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days into an evidence-based decision sequence rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days should be considered from four perspectives: resilience assessment, fault injection, steady state, and game day evidence. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 48—Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 3.4 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must continue operating and the acceptable level of loss; the application team describes traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform team translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days, resilience assessment is the entry point, fault injection reveals how the system responds, and steady state plus game day evidence determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model applicable to every scenario is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but inappropriate for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 48 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundaries does the request cross, which identities are evaluated, where is the data retained, which components can fail, and what mechanisms restore them? If the story breaks at resilience assessment or fault injection, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/1/paragraphs/0: resilience assessment is the first element in decisions about Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days, but its value emerges only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what a service does. Identify the inputs received by resilience assessment, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the architecture has been declared resilient, but the recovery path has never been tested during an actual dependency failure. Connecting resilience assessment to fault injection reveals where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options lead to different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/1/paragraphs/1: fault injection is the second element in decisions about Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days, but its value emerges only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what a service does. Identify the inputs received by fault injection, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the architecture has been declared resilient, but the recovery path has never been tested during an actual dependency failure. Connecting fault injection to steady state reveals where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options lead to different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/1/paragraphs/2: steady state is the third element in decisions about Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days, but its value emerges only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what a service does. Identify the inputs received by steady state, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the architecture has been declared resilient, but the recovery path has never been tested during an actual dependency failure. Connecting steady state to game day evidence reveals where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options lead to different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/1/paragraphs/3: game day evidence is the fourth element in decisions about Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days, but its value emerges only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what a service does. Identify the inputs received by game day evidence, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the architecture has been declared resilient, but the recovery path has never been tested during an actual dependency failure. Connecting game day evidence to resilience assessment reveals where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options lead to different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving resilience assessment should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm the mandatory requirements, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare resilience assessment with steady state using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 48, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving fault injection should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm the mandatory requirements, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare fault injection with game day evidence using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 48, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving steady state should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm the mandatory requirements, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare steady state with resilience assessment using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 48, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving game day evidence should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm the mandatory requirements, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare game day evidence with fault injection using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 48, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/3/title: Design for failure before failure occurs
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days occurs when the project team assumes that resilience assessment automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of resilience assessment and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that an answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days occurs when the project team assumes that fault injection automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of fault injection and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that an answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days occurs when the project team assumes that steady state automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of steady state and identify what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that an answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 48, the architecture has been declared resilient, but the recovery path has never been tested during an actual dependency failure. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a consistent experience, security needs boundaries and audit trails, finance needs cost attribution, and product teams need to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on resilience assessment, fault injection, and steady state. Each option is scored using the same matrix: mandatory requirement coverage, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. game day evidence serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be reconsidered.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not satisfy a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days, mark every detail related to resilience assessment and fault injection. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the right service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a completely different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “cost priority.” In Lesson 48, use steady state to validate the assurance level and game day evidence to determine whether the operations team can sustain the solution. An answer that is functionally correct but provides the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 48—Resilience Hub, FIS, and game days—objective SAP-C02 3.4 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

sap-48-resilience-hub-fis-game-day./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to verify coverage—Lesson 48

sap-49-performance-improvement./summary: Understand how to find bottlenecks and improve performance through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-49-performance-improvement./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and the team increases resource sizes after every incident, yet latency remains high because the bottleneck lies in another dependency. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it effectively. Lesson 49 begins with this scenario and turns finding bottlenecks and improving performance into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-49-performance-improvement./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Finding bottlenecks and improving performance should be considered from four perspectives: load profile, bottleneck evidence, scaling limit, and performance efficiency. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-49-performance-improvement./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 49—Finding Bottlenecks and Improving Performance—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 3.3 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/0/title: Thinking Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. When finding bottlenecks and improving performance, the load profile is the starting point, bottleneck evidence shows how the system responds, and scaling limits and performance efficiency determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for any lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. When R-C-D-E is applied to finding bottlenecks and improving performance, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 49 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the load profile or bottleneck evidence, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Be Connected
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The load profile is the first piece of the decision about finding bottlenecks and improving performance, but it becomes valuable only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by the load profile, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the team increases resource sizes after every incident, yet latency remains high because the bottleneck lies in another dependency. Connecting the load profile to bottleneck evidence reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Bottleneck evidence is the second piece of the decision about finding bottlenecks and improving performance, but it becomes valuable only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by bottleneck evidence, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the team increases resource sizes after every incident, yet latency remains high because the bottleneck lies in another dependency. Connecting bottleneck evidence to the scaling limit reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The scaling limit is the third piece of the decision about finding bottlenecks and improving performance, but it becomes valuable only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by the scaling limit, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the team increases resource sizes after every incident, yet latency remains high because the bottleneck lies in another dependency. Connecting the scaling limit to performance efficiency reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Performance efficiency is the fourth piece of the decision about finding bottlenecks and improving performance, but it becomes valuable only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the inputs received by performance efficiency, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the team increases resource sizes after every incident, yet latency remains high because the bottleneck lies in another dependency. sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Record the Trade-offs
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving the load profile should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the load profile with the scaling limit using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 49, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving bottleneck evidence should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare bottleneck evidence with performance efficiency using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 49, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving the scaling limit should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the scaling limit with the load profile using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 49, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving performance efficiency should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare performance efficiency with bottleneck evidence using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 49, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before It Occurs
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in finding bottlenecks and improving performance occurs when the project team assumes that the load profile automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the load profile and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in finding bottlenecks and improving performance occurs when the project team assumes that bottleneck evidence automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of bottleneck evidence and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in finding bottlenecks and improving performance occurs when the project team assumes that the scaling limit automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of the scaling limit and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 49, the team increases resource sizes after every incident, yet latency remains high because the bottleneck lies in another dependency. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the load profile, bottleneck evidence, and scaling limit. Each option is scored using the same matrix: compliance with mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. Performance efficiency serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for finding bottlenecks and improving performance is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/5/title: Choosing the Right Action in SAP-C02 Questions
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For finding bottlenecks and improving performance, mark every detail related to the load profile and bottleneck evidence. Then classify each answer as fully compliant, partially compliant, introducing additional risk, or out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it names the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a completely different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “cost priority.” In Lesson 49, use the scaling limit to validate the level of assurance and performance efficiency to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong level of assurance is still a distractor.
sap-49-performance-improvement./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 49—Finding Bottlenecks and Improving Performance—objective SAP-C02 3.3 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the evidence and select the cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

sap-49-performance-improvement./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the load profile and the responsibility of bottleneck evidence.
The option must include a failure mode and a recovery path related to the scaling limit.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess performance efficiency.

sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/title: Architecture Review: Finding Bottlenecks and Improving Performance
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the team increases resource sizes after every incident, yet latency remains high because the bottleneck lies in another dependency. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs a cost commitment.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly explain the trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are load profile → bottleneck evidence → scaling limit → performance efficiency.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the load profile.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must reflect the role of bottleneck evidence.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the scaling limit without relying on assumptions.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/constraints/1: The performance efficiency trade-off must be accepted by the stakeholders.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the load profile, bottleneck evidence, and scaling limit.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Map the request and data flow, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow directions.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the load profile and introduces no assumptions beyond the question.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Bottleneck evidence, the scaling limit, and performance efficiency are compared using the same criteria.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; no option is universally best.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the load profile; bottleneck evidence is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option that prioritizes the scaling limit is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of performance efficiency without adding value.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the load profile and bottleneck evidence, with the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric clearly documented.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which the team increases resource sizes after every incident, yet latency remains high because the bottleneck lies in another dependency.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the question eliminated the most options?
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-49-performance-improvement./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changed, which part of the architecture would need to change first?
sap-49-performance-improvement./recap/0: Finding bottlenecks and improving performance must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-49-performance-improvement./recap/1: The load profile, bottleneck evidence, scaling limit, and performance efficiency form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-49-performance-improvement./recap/2: Every decision needs a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-49-performance-improvement./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-49-performance-improvement./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of the load profile and bottleneck evidence?
sap-49-performance-improvement./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode is related to the scaling limit, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
sap-49-performance-improvement./selfCheck/2: Why can performance efficiency change the architectural choice?
sap-49-performance-improvement./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?

sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./summary: Understand portfolio-level cost optimization through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be clearly explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and finance wants to make a large commitment even though the workload has not been rightsized and cost ownership remains unclear. A diagram filled with AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 50 begins with this scenario and turns portfolio-level cost optimization into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” Portfolio-level cost optimization should be evaluated from four perspectives: cost and usage report, Compute Optimizer, commitment coverage, and unit cost. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 50 — Portfolio-level cost optimization is aligned with objective SAP-C02 3.5 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss. The application team describes traffic, data, and dependencies. The platform team translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For portfolio-level cost optimization, the cost and usage report is the entry point, compute optimizer shows how the system responds, and commitment coverage and unit cost determine long-term operational viability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome. A constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills. A decision is the selected architectural structure. Evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to portfolio-level cost optimization, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but inappropriate for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 50 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross? Which identity is evaluated? Where is the data retained? Which component can fail, and which mechanism restores it? If the story breaks at the cost and usage report or compute optimizer, the diagram still contains an unvalidated assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/1/paragraphs/0: cost and usage report is the first element in a portfolio-level cost optimization decision, but it provides value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the cost and usage report, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it incurs. At BlueRetail, finance wants to make a large commitment even though the workload has not been rightsized and cost ownership remains unclear. Connecting the cost and usage report with compute optimizer reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Compute Optimizer is the second element in a portfolio-level cost optimization decision, but it provides value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by compute optimizer, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it incurs. At BlueRetail, finance wants to make a large commitment even though the workload has not been rightsized and cost ownership remains unclear. Connecting compute optimizer with commitment coverage reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.

For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by commitment coverage, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it incurs. At BlueRetail, finance wants to make a large commitment even though the workload has not been rightsized and cost ownership remains unclear. Connecting commitment coverage with unit cost reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by unit cost, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it incurs. sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving the cost and usage report should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the cost and usage report with commitment coverage using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 50, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving compute optimizer should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare compute optimizer with unit cost using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 50, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving commitment coverage should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare commitment coverage with the cost and usage report using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 50, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving unit cost should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare unit cost with compute optimizer using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 50, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in portfolio-level cost optimization occurs when the project team assumes that the cost and usage report automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace proper access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the cost and usage report, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in portfolio-level cost optimization occurs when the project team assumes that compute optimizer automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace proper access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of compute optimizer, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in portfolio-level cost optimization occurs when the project team assumes that commitment coverage automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace proper access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of commitment coverage, marking what AWS is responsible for and what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 50, finance wants to make a large commitment even though the workload has not been rightsized and cost ownership remains unclear. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the cost and usage report, Compute Optimizer, and commitment coverage. Each option is scored using the same matrix: mandatory requirement coverage, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. unit cost serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The portfolio-level cost optimization conclusion is written in four sentences: which structure is selected, which requirements it satisfies, which trade-off is accepted, and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many appealing features.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/5/title: Selecting the right action in SAP-C02 questions
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the required outcome, the constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For portfolio-level cost optimization, mark every detail related to the cost and usage report and compute optimizer. Then classify the answers as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or falling outside the scope. Do not choose an answer simply because it mentions the correct service. AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful scrutiny. They may call for a very different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 50, use commitment coverage to verify the assurance level and unit cost to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 50 — Portfolio-level cost optimization, objective SAP-C02 3.5 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes. For determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option. For improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the cost and usage report and the responsibility of Compute Optimizer.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to commitment coverage.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess unit cost.

sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the keyword cost and usage report and immediately selecting the answer with the same service or feature name.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./commonMistakes/0/better: Evaluate the cost and usage report against the requirement, constraints, and compute optimizer before choosing.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 50, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the keyword Compute Optimizer and immediately selecting the answer with the same service or feature name.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./commonMistakes/1/better: Evaluate compute optimizer against the requirement, constraints, and commitment coverage before choosing.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 50, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the keyword commitment coverage and immediately selecting the answer with the same service or feature name.

sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAP-C02 3.5 to determine the required depth of the answer.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to the cost and usage report.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that merely shift risk from Compute Optimizer to commitment coverage without actually addressing it.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./examFocus/3: Select an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence based on unit cost.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because finance wants to make a large commitment even though the workload has not been rightsized and cost ownership remains unclear. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs a cost commitment.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly communicate trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are cost and usage report → Compute Optimizer → commitment coverage → unit cost.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the cost and usage report.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of Compute Optimizer.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe commitment coverage without relying on assumptions.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/constraints/1: The unit cost trade-off must be accepted by stakeholders.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the cost and usage report, Compute Optimizer, and commitment coverage.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flow, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow direction.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix containing at least two options.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the cost and usage report and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compute Optimizer, commitment coverage, and unit cost are compared using the same criteria.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing factors; there is no universally best option.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the cost and usage report; Compute Optimizer is a constraint that directly affects the selection.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing commitment coverage is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of unit cost without adding value.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the cost and usage report with Compute Optimizer and explicitly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which finance wants to make a large commitment even though the workload has not been rightsized and cost ownership remains unclear.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying that “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./recap/0: Portfolio-level cost optimization must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./recap/1: cost and usage report, Compute Optimizer, commitment coverage, and unit cost form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./recap/2: Every decision needs a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of the cost and usage report and Compute Optimizer?
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode is related to commitment coverage, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./selfCheck/2: Why can unit cost change the architectural choice?
sap-50-cost-optimization-portfolio./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?
