sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./title: Discovery and assessment for an application portfolio
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./shortTitle: Discovery and assessment for an application portfolio
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./summary: Understand discovery and assessment for an application portfolio through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and hundreds of servers have been listed but not yet grouped by application, owner, and dependency. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 53 starts with this scenario and turns discovery and assessment for an application portfolio into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” Discovery and assessment for an application portfolio should be considered from four perspectives: application portfolio, dependency discovery, business criticality, and migration readiness. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if they conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 53 — Discovery and assessment for an application portfolio is aligned with objective SAP-C02 4.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are cross-checked against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose” but also “why the other options were not chosen.”
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business specifies what must remain operational and what level of loss is acceptable; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For discovery and assessment of an application portfolio, the application portfolio is the entry point, dependency discovery reveals how the system behaves, and business criticality and migration readiness determine long-term operational viability. Going directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that can be used for every exercise is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to discovery and assessment for an application portfolio, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/0/paragraphs/2: A sign that you understand Lesson 53 is that you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the application portfolio or dependency discovery stage, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The application portfolio is the first component in a discovery and assessment decision for an application portfolio, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the application portfolio, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, hundreds of servers have been listed but not yet grouped by application, owner, and dependency. Connecting the application portfolio with dependency discovery reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Dependency discovery is the second component in a discovery and assessment decision for an application portfolio, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by dependency discovery, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, hundreds of servers have been listed but not yet grouped by application, owner, and dependency. Connecting dependency discovery with business criticality reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/1/paragraphs/2: Business criticality is the third component in a discovery and assessment decision for an application portfolio, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by business criticality, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, hundreds of servers have been listed but not yet grouped by application, owner, and dependency. Connecting business criticality with migration readiness reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Migration readiness is the fourth component in a discovery and assessment decision for an application portfolio, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by migration readiness, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, hundreds of servers have been listed but not yet grouped by application, owner, and dependency. Connecting migration readiness with the application portfolio reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the capabilities of the operations team.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving the application portfolio should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the application portfolio with business criticality using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 53, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving dependency discovery should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare dependency discovery with migration readiness using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 53, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving business criticality should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare business criticality with the application portfolio using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 53, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving migration readiness should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare migration readiness with dependency discovery using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 53, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in discovery and assessment for an application portfolio occurs when the project team assumes that the application portfolio automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the application portfolio, marking the portions for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in discovery and assessment for an application portfolio occurs when the project team assumes that dependency discovery automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of dependency discovery, marking the portions for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in discovery and assessment for an application portfolio occurs when the project team assumes that business criticality automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of business criticality, marking the portions for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 53, hundreds of servers have been listed but not yet grouped by application, owner, and dependency. The first step is to distinguish the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and audit trails, finance needs cost attribution, and product teams need to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder will create operational debt for another.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the application portfolio, dependency discovery, and business criticality. Each option is scored using the same matrix: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of use. Migration readiness serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for discovery and assessment for an application portfolio is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it fulfills; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations share the same understanding of a decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails to meet a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/5/title: How to choose an action in SAP-C02 questions
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the mandatory outcome, the constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For discovery and assessment for an application portfolio, mark every detail related to the application portfolio and dependency discovery. Then classify each answer as fully meeting the requirements, partially meeting them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer simply because it names the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may call for designs that differ significantly from those associated with “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 53, use business criticality to validate the required assurance level and migration readiness to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that offers the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 53 — Discovery and assessment for an application portfolio, objective SAP-C02 4.1 indicates the required depth of understanding. For the verb design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option; and for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. Conclude each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./visuals/0/title: Decision map: Discovery and assessment for an application portfolio
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to the application portfolio, dependency discovery, business criticality, and migration readiness in the BlueRetail case.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision analysis for discovery and assessment for an application portfolio
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the situation in which “hundreds of servers have been listed but not yet grouped by application, owner, and dependency” while still being able to explain the impact on security, recoverability, performance, and cost.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the application portfolio and the responsibilities of dependency discovery.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to business criticality.
The operations team must use actual data to observe and reassess migration readiness.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the application portfolio perspective in step 1.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address dependency discovery on its own, and the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the dependency discovery perspective in step 2.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address business criticality on its own, and the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the business criticality perspective in step 3.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address migration readiness on its own, and the remaining responsibilities must be reviewed.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines the application portfolio with dependency discovery, using business criticality as a guardrail and migration readiness as operational evidence.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare the options using the same quality attributes and time horizon.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would trigger reconsideration of the decision.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize the application portfolio when the primary requirement is associated with step 1 of the decision.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./comparison/0/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses the application portfolio to address the situation in which hundreds of servers have been listed but not yet grouped by application, owner, and dependency.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./comparison/0/tradeOff: Dependency discovery must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./comparison/1/bestFit: Prioritize dependency discovery when the primary requirement is associated with step 2 of the decision.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses dependency discovery to address the situation in which hundreds of servers have been listed but not yet grouped by application, owner, and dependency.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./comparison/1/tradeOff: Business criticality must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./comparison/2/bestFit: Prioritize business criticality when the primary requirement is associated with step 3 of the decision.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./comparison/2/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses business criticality to address the situation in which hundreds of servers have been listed but not yet grouped by application, owner, and dependency.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./comparison/2/tradeOff: Migration readiness must be added or validated; do not treat this as a solution for every layer.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the keyword application portfolio and immediately selecting the answer with the same service or feature name.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./commonMistakes/0/better: Evaluate the application portfolio against the requirements, constraints, and dependency discovery before making a selection.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 53, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibility and all failure modes are addressed.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the keyword dependency discovery and immediately selecting the answer with the same service or feature name.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./commonMistakes/1/better: Evaluate dependency discovery against the requirements, constraints, and business criticality before making a selection.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 53, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibility and all failure modes are addressed.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the keyword business criticality and immediately selecting the answer with the same service or feature name.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./commonMistakes/2/better: Evaluate business criticality against the requirements, constraints, and migration readiness before making a selection.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./commonMistakes/2/reason: In Lesson 53, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibility and all failure modes are addressed.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAP-C02 4.1 to determine the required depth of the answer.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to the application portfolio.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that transfer risk from dependency discovery to business criticality without actually addressing it.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./examFocus/3: Select an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence provided through migration readiness.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/title: Architecture review: Discovery and assessment for an application portfolio
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because hundreds of servers have been listed but not yet grouped by application, owner, and dependency. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs cost commitments.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly explain the trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are application portfolio → dependency discovery → business criticality → migration readiness.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the application portfolio.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of dependency discovery.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe business criticality without relying on assumptions.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to obscure a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/constraints/1: The migration readiness trade-off must be accepted by the stakeholders.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the application portfolio, dependency discovery, and business criticality.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flows, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow directions.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the application portfolio and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or a single keyword.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares dependency discovery, business criticality, and migration readiness using the same criteria.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing competing factors; no option is absolutely best.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after implementation.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the application portfolio; dependency discovery is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option that prioritizes business criticality is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of migration readiness without adding value.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the application portfolio and dependency discovery and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-53-portfolio-discovery-assessment./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which hundreds of servers have been listed but not yet grouped by application, owner, and dependency.

sap-54-seven-r-strategy./title: Choose a 7R Strategy for Each Workload
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./shortTitle: Choose a 7R Strategy for Each Workload
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./summary: Learn how to choose a 7R strategy for each workload by evaluating requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./opening/0: BlueRetail is transforming its architecture, and the program office wants to apply a single migration strategy even though the applications differ in age, value, and constraints. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 54 begins with this scenario and turns the process of choosing a 7R strategy for each workload into an evidence-based sequence of decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically viable options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” Choosing a 7R strategy for each workload requires evaluating four perspectives: rehost, replatform, refactor, and retire and retain. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 54—Choose a 7R Strategy for Each Workload—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 4.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss. The application defines traffic, data, and dependencies. The platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. When choosing a 7R strategy for each workload, rehost is the entry point, replatform defines how the system responds, and refactor and retire and retain determine its long-term operability. Moving directly from requirements to service names often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that works for any scenario is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the desired outcome. A constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills. A decision is the selected architectural structure. Evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E when choosing a 7R strategy for each workload, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but unsuitable for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 54 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross? Which identity is evaluated? Where is the data stored? Which component can fail, and which mechanism restores it? If the narrative breaks at rehost or replatform, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption next to the diagram before selecting additional services.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/1/paragraphs/0: rehost is the first element in the decision about choosing a 7R strategy for each workload, but its value becomes clear only when it is evaluated against business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that rehost receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the program office wants to apply a single migration strategy even though the applications differ in age, value, and constraints. Connecting rehost with replatform reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/1/paragraphs/1: replatform is the second element in the decision about choosing a 7R strategy for each workload, but its value becomes clear only when it is evaluated against business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that replatform receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the program office wants to apply a single migration strategy even though the applications differ in age, value, and constraints. Connecting replatform with refactor reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/1/paragraphs/2: refactor is the third element in the decision about choosing a 7R strategy for each workload, but its value becomes clear only when it is evaluated against business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that refactor receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the program office wants to apply a single migration strategy even though the applications differ in age, value, and constraints. Connecting refactor with retire and retain reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/1/paragraphs/3: retire and retain is the fourth element in the decision about choosing a 7R strategy for each workload, but its value becomes clear only when it is evaluated against business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that retire and retain receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the program office wants to apply a single migration strategy even though the applications differ in age, value, and constraints. Connecting retire and retain with rehost reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving rehost should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility model limitations. Next, compare rehost with refactor using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 54, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that should trigger an architecture review.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving replatform should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility model limitations. Next, compare replatform with retire and retain using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 54, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that should trigger an architecture review.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving refactor should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility model limitations. Next, compare refactor with rehost using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 54, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that should trigger an architecture review.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving retire and retain should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility model limitations. Next, compare retire and retain with replatform using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 54, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and identify the signals that should trigger an architecture review.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 when choosing a 7R strategy for each workload occurs when the project team assumes that rehost automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of rehost and mark which parts are AWS responsibilities and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 when choosing a 7R strategy for each workload occurs when the project team assumes that replatform automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of replatform and mark which parts are AWS responsibilities and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 when choosing a 7R strategy for each workload occurs when the project team assumes that refactor automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of refactor and mark which parts are AWS responsibilities and which parts the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer works not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 54, the program office wants to apply a single migration strategy even though the applications differ in age, value, and constraints. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and product teams need to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on rehost, replatform, and refactor. Each option is scored against the same criteria: mandatory requirement coverage, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. retire and retain serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the team must revisit the constraints.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for choosing a 7R strategy for each workload is written in four sentences: which structure is selected, which requirements it meets, which trade-off is accepted, and which metric or review will trigger a reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations share the same understanding of the decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not meet a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/5/title: How to choose an action in SAP-C02 questions
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. When choosing a 7R strategy for each workload, mark every detail related to rehost and replatform. Then classify each answer as fully compliant, partially compliant, introducing additional risk, or out of scope. Do not select an answer merely because it mentions the correct service. AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may require a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 54, use refactor to validate the required assurance level and retire and retain to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the correct feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 54—Choose a 7R Strategy for Each Workload—objective SAP-C02 4.1 indicates the required depth of understanding. For design, you must balance quality attributes. For determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option. For improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

sap-54-seven-r-strategy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the situation in which “the program office wants to apply a single migration strategy even though the applications differ in age, value, and constraints,” while still being able to explain the impact on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of rehost and the responsibilities of replatform.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to refactor.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess retire and retain.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the rehost perspective at step 1.

sap-54-seven-r-strategy./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the keyword rehost and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./commonMistakes/0/better: Evaluate rehost against the requirements, constraints, and replatform before making a selection.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 54, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the keyword replatform and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./commonMistakes/1/better: Evaluate replatform against the requirements, constraints, and refactor before making a selection.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 54, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the keyword refactor and immediately selecting an answer with the same service or feature name.

sap-54-seven-r-strategy./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAP-C02 4.1 to determine the required depth of the answer.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to rehost.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that transfer risk from replatform to refactor without actually addressing it.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./examFocus/3: Choose an option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence provided through retire and retain.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/title: Architecture review: Choose a 7R Strategy for Each Workload
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the program office wants to apply a single migration strategy even though the applications differ in age, value, and constraints. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs cost commitments.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly explain the trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are rehost → replatform → refactor → retire and retain.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to rehost.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of replatform.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe refactor without relying on assumptions.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off related to retire and retain.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on rehost, replatform, and refactor.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Map the request and data flow, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to rehost and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares replatform, refactor, and retire and retain using the same criteria.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing trade-offs; no option is universally best.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validation metric or test.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after implementation.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around rehost; replatform is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option that prioritizes refactor is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of retire and retain without adding value.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines rehost and replatform and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which the program office wants to apply a single migration strategy even though the applications differ in age, value, and constraints.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/commonMistakes/2: Claiming that “AWS manages it automatically” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reassess the decision?
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./recap/0: Choosing a 7R strategy for each workload must begin with requirements and constraints.

sap-54-seven-r-strategy./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of rehost and replatform?
sap-54-seven-r-strategy./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode is related to refactor, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
