sap-58-migration-network-cutover./title: Network, DNS, and cutover in migration
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./shortTitle: Network, DNS, and cutover in migration
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./summary: Understand network, DNS, and cutover in migration through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, with applications running concurrently on-premises and on AWS, so routes, firewalls, and DNS must support the coexistence phase. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 58 begins with this scenario and turns network, DNS, and cutover in migration into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no use of the public internet.” Network, DNS, and cutover in migration should be evaluated from four perspectives: connectivity readiness, DNS cutover, route coexistence, and rollback path. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 58—Network, DNS, and cutover in migration—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 4.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must continue operating and the acceptable level of loss. The application defines traffic, data, and dependencies. The platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For network, DNS, and cutover in migration, connectivity readiness is the entry point, DNS cutover determines how the system responds, and route coexistence and the rollback path determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome. A constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills. A decision is the selected architectural structure. Evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to Network, DNS, and cutover in migration, learners can see that the same service may be correct for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You understand Lesson 58 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundaries does the request cross? Which identities are evaluated? Where is the data retained? Which components can fail, and which mechanisms provide recovery? If the story breaks at connectivity readiness or DNS cutover, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/1/paragraphs/0: connectivity readiness is the first element in decisions about network, DNS, and cutover in migration, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what a service does. Identify the inputs connectivity readiness receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, applications run concurrently on-premises and on AWS, so routes, firewalls, and DNS must support the coexistence phase. Connecting connectivity readiness with DNS cutover reveals where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/1/paragraphs/1: DNS cutover is the second element in decisions about network, DNS, and cutover in migration, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what a service does. Identify the inputs DNS cutover receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, applications run concurrently on-premises and on AWS, so routes, firewalls, and DNS must support the coexistence phase. Connecting DNS cutover with route coexistence reveals where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/1/paragraphs/2: route coexistence is the third element in decisions about network, DNS, and cutover in migration, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what a service does. Identify the inputs route coexistence receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, applications run concurrently on-premises and on AWS, so routes, firewalls, and DNS must support the coexistence phase. Connecting route coexistence with the rollback path reveals where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/1/paragraphs/3: rollback path is the fourth element in decisions about network, DNS, and cutover in migration, but its value becomes apparent only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking what a service does. Identify the inputs the rollback path receives, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the operational costs it introduces. At BlueRetail, applications run concurrently on-premises and on AWS, so routes, firewalls, and DNS must support the coexistence phase. Connecting the rollback path with connectivity readiness reveals where responsibility boundaries lie and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving connectivity readiness should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. Next, compare connectivity readiness with route coexistence using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and explain how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 58, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture must be reassessed.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving DNS cutover should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. Next, compare DNS cutover with the rollback path using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and explain how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 58, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture must be reassessed.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving route coexistence should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. Next, compare route coexistence with connectivity readiness using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and explain how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 58, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture must be reassessed.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving the rollback path should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory. Then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network paths, and the responsibility model. Next, compare the rollback path with DNS cutover using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and explain how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 58, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough for the current context and which signals would indicate that the architecture must be reassessed.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 for network, DNS, and cutover in migration occurs when the project team assumes that connectivity readiness automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of connectivity readiness and identify the portions for which AWS is responsible and those the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 for network, DNS, and cutover in migration occurs when the project team assumes that DNS cutover automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of DNS cutover and identify the portions for which AWS is responsible and those the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 for network, DNS, and cutover in migration occurs when the project team assumes that route coexistence automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of route coexistence and identify the portions for which AWS is responsible and those the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 58, applications run concurrently on-premises and on AWS, so routes, firewalls, and DNS must support the coexistence phase. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on connectivity readiness, DNS cutover, and route coexistence. Each option is scored against the same criteria: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. The rollback path serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for network, DNS, and cutover in migration is written as four statements: which structure is selected, which requirements it satisfies, which trade-offs are accepted, and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations share the same understanding of the decision. In exam questions, these four statements also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not satisfy a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/5/title: How to select an action in SAP-C02 questions
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For network, DNS, and cutover in migration, mark every detail related to connectivity readiness and DNS cutover. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not select an answer merely because it mentions the correct service. AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may call for a completely different design than terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 58, use route coexistence to evaluate the required assurance level and the rollback path to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the correct feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-58-migration-network-cutover./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 58—Network, DNS, and cutover in migration—objective SAP-C02 4.1 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes. For determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option. For improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

sap-59-migration-wave-planning./title: Wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./shortTitle: Wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./summary: Understand wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria through the requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an explainable architecture decision for the BlueRetail team.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, but its program groups servers by department while ignoring technical dependencies, preventing the first wave from operating independently. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 59 begins with this scenario and turns wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria should be evaluated from four perspectives: migration wave, dependency group, cutover runbook, and exit criteria. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers if they conflict with a requirement or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 59 — Wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria is aligned with objective SAP-C02 4.1 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why not to choose the other options.”
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must continue operating and what level of loss is acceptable; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria, the migration wave is the entry point, the dependency group shows how the system responds, and the cutover runbook and exit criteria determine long-term operability. Moving directly from requirements to service names often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that applies to every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architecture structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria, learners can see that the same service may be correct for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/0/paragraphs/2: A sign that you understand Lesson 59 is that you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the migration wave or dependency group, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/1/paragraphs/0: A migration wave is the first element in decisions about wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria, but it provides value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the migration wave, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the program groups servers by department while ignoring technical dependencies, preventing the first wave from operating independently. Connecting the migration wave to the dependency group reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/1/paragraphs/1: A dependency group is the second element in decisions about wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria, but it provides value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the dependency group, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the program groups servers by department while ignoring technical dependencies, preventing the first wave from operating independently. Connecting the dependency group to the cutover runbook reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/1/paragraphs/2: A cutover runbook is the third element in decisions about wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria, but it provides value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the cutover runbook, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the program groups servers by department while ignoring technical dependencies, preventing the first wave from operating independently. Connecting the cutover runbook to the exit criteria reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Exit criteria are the fourth element in decisions about wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria, but they provide value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the exit criteria, the state or policy they govern, the failure mode they address, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the program groups servers by department while ignoring technical dependencies, preventing the first wave from operating independently. Connecting the exit criteria to the migration wave reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving a migration wave should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then examine quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the migration wave with the cutover runbook using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 59, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving a dependency group should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then examine quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the dependency group with the exit criteria using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 59, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving a cutover runbook should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then examine quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the cutover runbook with the migration wave using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 59, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving exit criteria should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, and then examine quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the exit criteria with the dependency group using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 59, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria occurs when the project team assumes that the migration wave automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the migration wave, identifying the portion for which AWS is responsible and the portion the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria occurs when the project team assumes that the dependency group automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the dependency group, identifying the portion for which AWS is responsible and the portion the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria occurs when the project team assumes that the cutover runbook automatically addresses every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the cutover runbook, identifying the portion for which AWS is responsible and the portion the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only when the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 59, the program groups servers by department while ignoring technical dependencies, preventing the first wave from operating independently. The first step is to distinguish the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the migration wave, dependency group, and cutover runbook. Each option is scored using the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of use. The exit criteria serve as the final validation conditions. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the team must revisit the constraints.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reevaluation. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read a SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, required outcome, constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria, mark every detail related to the migration wave and dependency group. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or falling outside the scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may require a fundamentally different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 59, use the cutover runbook to verify the assurance level and the exit criteria to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that selects the correct feature but provides the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 59 — Wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria, objective SAP-C02 4.1 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; and for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architecture decision.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./visuals/0/title: Decision map: Wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to the migration wave, dependency group, cutover runbook, and exit criteria in the BlueRetail case.

sap-59-migration-wave-planning./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the situation in which “the program groups servers by department while ignoring technical dependencies, preventing the first wave from operating independently,” while still explaining the impact on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the migration wave and the responsibility of the dependency group.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to the cutover runbook.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reevaluate the exit criteria.

sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/title: Architecture review: Wave planning, dependencies, and exit criteria
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the program groups servers by department while ignoring technical dependencies, preventing the first wave from operating independently. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs cost commitments.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly communicate trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are migration wave → dependency group → cutover runbook → exit criteria.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the migration wave.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must reflect the role of the dependency group.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the cutover runbook without relying on assumptions.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off involving the exit criteria.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the migration wave, dependency group, and cutover runbook.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unconfirmed assumptions.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flows, including trust boundaries and blast radius.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow direction.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the migration wave and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting services based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/rubric/1/evidence: The dependency group, cutover runbook, and exit criteria are compared using the same criteria.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; no option is universally best.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The response identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and validated after implementation.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the migration wave; the dependency group is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option that prioritizes the cutover runbook is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of the exit criteria without adding value.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the migration wave and dependency group and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-59-migration-wave-planning./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which the program groups servers by department while ignoring technical dependencies, preventing the first wave from operating independently.

sap-59-migration-wave-planning./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to verify coverage — Lesson 59

sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./title: Modernize with containers, serverless, and events
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./shortTitle: Modernize with containers, serverless, and events
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./summary: Understand modernization with containers, serverless, and events through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and after rehosting, the team wants to convert every application to microservices despite lacking suitable business objectives or ownership. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design. What matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 60 starts with this scenario and turns modernization with containers, serverless, and events into a sequence of evidence-based decisions instead of a list of features to memorize.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Modernization with containers, serverless, and events must be evaluated from four perspectives: modernization outcome, container boundary, serverless event, and operational burden. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 60—Modernize with containers, serverless, and events—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 4.2 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic inventory is used only to verify coverage and content organization. All explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options were not chosen.”
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of an AWS architecture as a contract between business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For modernization with containers, serverless, and events, modernization outcome is the entry point, container boundary determines how the system responds, and serverless event and operational burden determine long-term operability. Going directly from requirements to service names often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. Requirement describes the outcome; constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to Modernize with containers, serverless, and events, learners can see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 60 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the modernization outcome or container boundary, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/1/paragraphs/0: modernization outcome is the first building block in a decision to modernize with containers, serverless, and events, but its value emerges only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input received by the modernization outcome, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it addresses, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, after rehosting, the team wants to convert every application to microservices despite lacking suitable business objectives or ownership. Connecting modernization outcome to container boundary reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different results. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/1/paragraphs/1: container boundary is the second building block in a decision to modernize with containers, serverless, and events, but its value emerges only when considered alongside business requirements. sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving modernization outcome should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare modernization outcome with serverless event using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 60, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving container boundary should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare container boundary with operational burden using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 60, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.

First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then examine quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare operational burden with container boundary using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in modernization with containers, serverless, and events occurs when the project team assumes that modernization outcome automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the modernization outcome, mark what AWS is responsible for, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in modernization with containers, serverless, and events occurs when the project team assumes that container boundary automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the container boundary, mark what AWS is responsible for, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in modernization with containers, serverless, and events occurs when the project team assumes that serverless event automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of the serverless event, mark what AWS is responsible for, and identify what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 60, after rehosting, the team wants to convert every application to microservices despite lacking suitable business objectives or ownership. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and product teams need to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on modernization outcome, container boundary, and serverless event. Each option is scored using the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of consumption. operational burden serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for modernization with containers, serverless, and events is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/5/title: Think in terms of selecting actions in SAP-C02 questions
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read each SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, required outcome, constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For modernization with containers, serverless, and events, mark every detail related to modernization outcome and container boundary. Then classify each answer as fully compliant, partially compliant, introducing additional risk, or out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it names the right service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may require a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 60, use serverless event to evaluate the assurance level and operational burden to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 60—Modernize with containers, serverless, and events—objective SAP-C02 4.2 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. Conclude each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.

The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess operational burden.

sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/title: Architecture review: Modernize with containers, serverless, and events
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because, after rehosting, the team wants to convert every application to microservices despite lacking suitable business objectives or ownership. You must review the architecture before the team begins implementation or incurs cost commitments.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly communicate trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are modernization outcome → container boundary → serverless event → operational burden.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to modernization outcome.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of container boundary.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe serverless event without relying on assumptions.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to mask an inadequately defined requirement.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/constraints/1: The operational burden trade-off must be accepted by stakeholders.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable evaluation criteria.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on modernization outcome, container boundary, and serverless event.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Draw the request and data flow, including the trust boundary and blast radius.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data flow direction.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix comparing at least two options.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing the trade-off and evidence.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to modernization outcome and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares container boundary, serverless event, and operational burden using the same criteria.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; no option is universally best.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validation metric or test.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around modernization outcome; container boundary is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option prioritizing serverless event is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of operational burden without adding value.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines modernization outcome and container boundary and explicitly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the scenario in which, after rehosting, the team wants to convert every application to microservices despite lacking suitable business objectives or ownership.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/commonMistakes/2: Claiming that “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./recap/0: Modernization with containers, serverless, and events must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./recap/1: modernization outcome, container boundary, serverless event, and operational burden form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./recap/2: Every decision needs a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.

sap-60-modernize-containers-serverless./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to verify coverage—Lesson 60

sap-61-post-migration-optimization./summary: Understand post-migration stabilization and optimization through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architectural decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and the workload has been cut over, but the source resources have not been shut down, sizing remains unchanged, and benefit kpis have not been measured. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 61 starts with this situation and turns post-migration stabilization and optimization into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” Post-migration stabilization and optimization should be evaluated from four perspectives: stabilization period, rightsizing, decommission evidence, and benefit realization. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 61—Post-migration Stabilization and Optimization—is aligned with objective SAP-C02 4.2 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/0/title: Thinking model: start with requirements, not services
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For post-migration stabilization and optimization, the stabilization period is the entry point, rightsizing shows how the system responds, and decommission evidence and benefit realization determine long-term operability. Moving directly from requirements to service names often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that works for every lesson is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence consists of metrics, logs, tests, or documentation confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to post-migration stabilization and optimization, learners can see why the same service may be correct for one workload but wrong for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 61 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the stabilization period or rightsizing, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/1/title: Four concepts that must be connected
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/1/paragraphs/0: stabilization period is the 1st element in a post-migration stabilization and optimization decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What does this service do?” Identify the inputs received by stabilization period, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the workload has been cut over, but the source resources have not been shut down, sizing remains unchanged, and benefit kpis have not been measured. Connecting stabilization period with rightsizing reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/1/paragraphs/1: rightsizing is the 2nd element in a post-migration stabilization and optimization decision, but its value becomes clear only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What does this service do?” Identify the inputs received by rightsizing, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. Connecting rightsizing with decommission evidence reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.

For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What does this service do?” Identify the inputs received by decommission evidence, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the workload has been cut over, but the source resources have not been shut down, sizing remains unchanged, and benefit kpis have not been measured. Connecting decommission evidence with benefit realization reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options can produce different outcomes. A good solution must be traceable from the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.

For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What does this service do?” Identify the inputs received by benefit realization, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the workload has been cut over, but the source resources have not been shut down, sizing remains unchanged, and benefit kpis have not been measured. sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving stabilization period should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare stabilization period with decommission evidence using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 61, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving rightsizing should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare rightsizing with benefit realization using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 61, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving decommission evidence should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare decommission evidence with stabilization period using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 61, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving benefit realization should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare benefit realization with rightsizing using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 61, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architectural review.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in post-migration stabilization and optimization occurs when the project team assumes that stabilization period automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of stabilization period, identify the portion for which AWS is responsible, and mark what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in post-migration stabilization and optimization occurs when the project team assumes that rightsizing automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of rightsizing, identify the portion for which AWS is responsible, and mark what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in post-migration stabilization and optimization occurs when the project team assumes that decommission evidence automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backup, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, map the blast radius of decommission evidence, identify the portion for which AWS is responsible, and mark what the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer works not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 61, the workload has been cut over, but the source resources have not been shut down, sizing remains unchanged, and benefit KPI values have not been measured. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs rapid change. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on stabilization period, rightsizing, and decommission evidence. Each option is scored against the same criteria: mandatory requirement compliance, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of consumption. benefit realization serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the team must revisit the constraint.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for post-migration stabilization and optimization is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/5/title: How to select an action in SAP-C02 questions
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For post-migration stabilization and optimization, mark every detail related to stabilization period and rightsizing. Then classify each answer as fully compliant, partially compliant, introducing additional risk, or out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may require a completely different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “cost-optimized.” In Lesson 61, use decommission evidence to verify the assurance level and benefit realization to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that is functionally correct but provides the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 61—Post-migration Stabilization and Optimization—objective SAP-C02 4.2 indicates the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architectural decision.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./visuals/0/title: Decision map: Post-migration stabilization and optimization
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements with stabilization period, rightsizing, decommission evidence, and benefit realization in the BlueRetail case.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Post-migration stabilization and optimization decision analysis
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the situation in which “the workload has been cut over, but the source resources have not been shut down, sizing remains unchanged, and benefit KPI values have not been measured,” while still being able to explain the impact on security, resilience, performance, and cost.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of stabilization period and the responsibilities of rightsizing.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to decommission evidence.
The operations team must observe and reassess benefit realization using real data.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the stabilization period perspective in step 1.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not address rightsizing by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be verified.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Appropriate when the requirement prioritizes the rightsizing perspective in step 2.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/lim: Does not address decommission evidence by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be verified.
