sap-61-post-migration-optimization./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/2/lim: Does not address benefit realization by itself, and the remaining responsibilities must be verified.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./decisionWalkthroughs/0/decision: Select an option that combines stabilization period with rightsizing, uses decommission evidence as a guardrail, and uses benefit realization as operational evidence.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/0: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing costs.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/1: Compare options using the same quality attributes and the same time horizon.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/2: Clearly document the trade-off, blast radius, and responsibilities that remain with the application team.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would trigger reassessment of the decision.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./comparison/0/bestFit: Prioritize stabilization period when the primary requirement is associated with step 1 of the decision.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./comparison/0/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses stabilization period to address the situation in which the workload has been cut over, but the source resources have not been shut down, sizing remains unchanged, and benefit kpis have not been measured.

sap-61-post-migration-optimization./comparison/1/blueRetailUse: BlueRetail uses rightsizing to address the situation in which the workload has been cut over, but the source resources have not been shut down, sizing remains unchanged, and benefit kpis have not been measured.

sap-61-post-migration-optimization./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the keyword stabilization period and immediately selecting the answer with a service or feature of the same name.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./commonMistakes/0/better: Compare stabilization period against the requirement, constraint, and rightsizing before making a selection.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 61, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the keyword rightsizing and immediately selecting the answer with a service or feature of the same name.

sap-61-post-migration-optimization./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 61, a choice is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.

sap-61-post-migration-optimization./examFocus/0: Read the verb in objective SAP-C02 4.2 to determine the required depth of the answer.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to stabilization period.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that shift risk from rightsizing to decommission evidence without actually addressing it.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./examFocus/3: Select the option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence provided through benefit realization.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/title: Architecture review: Post-migration stabilization and optimization
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the workload has been cut over, but the source resources have not been shut down, sizing remains unchanged, and benefit kpis have not been measured. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs a cost commitment.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly communicate trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are stabilization period → rightsizing → decommission evidence → benefit realization.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to stabilization period.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must reflect the role of rightsizing.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe decommission evidence without relying on assumptions.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to mask an inadequately defined requirement.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/constraints/1: The trade-off involving benefit realization must be accepted by stakeholders.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as assessable criteria.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on stabilization period, rightsizing, and decommission evidence.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unverified assumptions.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Map the request and data flows together with trust boundaries and the blast radius.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow directions.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix containing at least two options.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to stabilization period and adds no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents service selection based on preference or a single keyword.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares rightsizing, decommission evidence, and benefit realization using the same criteria.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balance; there is no universally best option.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after implementation.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around stabilization period; rightsizing is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option prioritizing decommission evidence is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of benefit realization without adding value.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines stabilization period and rightsizing and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which the workload has been cut over, but the source resources have not been shut down, sizing remains unchanged, and benefit kpis have not been measured.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/commonMistakes/2: Saying “AWS manages it automatically” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reconsider the decision?
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./recap/0: Post-migration stabilization and optimization must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./recap/1: stabilization period, rightsizing, decommission evidence, and benefit realization form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of stabilization period and rightsizing?
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode is associated with decommission evidence, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./selfCheck/2: Why can benefit realization change the architectural choice?
sap-61-post-migration-optimization./selfCheck/3: Which metric, log, or test would you use to validate the decision?

sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./summary: Understand the capstone on designing global BlueRetail through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architecture decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./opening/0: BlueRetail is transforming its architecture and needs a global architecture that connects subsidiaries, storefronts, the data platform, and shared services. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 62 begins with this scenario and turns the capstone on designing global BlueRetail into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” The capstone on designing global BlueRetail must be evaluated from four perspectives: enterprise requirement, global architecture, governance boundary, and resilience trade-off. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 62 — Capstone: designing global BlueRetail is aligned with the SAP-C02 Domains 1–2 objectives in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only what to choose, but also why the other options should not be chosen.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/0/title: Mental model: start with requirements, not services
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business specifies what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss; the application defines traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For the capstone on designing global BlueRetail, the enterprise requirement is the entry point, the global architecture defines how the system responds, and the governance boundary and resilience trade-off determine long-term operability. Moving directly from requirements to service names usually causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that applies to every exercise is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architectural structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to the capstone on designing global BlueRetail, learners can see why the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill matters more than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 62 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundaries does the request cross, which identities are evaluated, where is the data retained, which components can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at the enterprise requirement or global architecture, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting additional services.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The enterprise requirement is the first component of the capstone decision on designing global BlueRetail, but its value becomes clear only in the context of the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking what a service does. Identify the inputs received by the enterprise requirement, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. BlueRetail needs a global architecture that connects subsidiaries, storefronts, the data platform, and shared services. Connecting the enterprise requirement to the global architecture reveals where responsibility lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace each technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The global architecture is the second component of the capstone decision on designing global BlueRetail, but its value becomes clear only in the context of the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking what a service does. Identify the inputs received by the global architecture, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. BlueRetail needs a global architecture that connects subsidiaries, storefronts, the data platform, and shared services. Connecting the global architecture to the governance boundary reveals where responsibility lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace each technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The governance boundary is the third component of the capstone decision on designing global BlueRetail, but its value becomes clear only in the context of the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking what a service does. Identify the inputs received by the governance boundary, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. BlueRetail needs a global architecture that connects subsidiaries, storefronts, the data platform, and shared services. Connecting the governance boundary to the resilience trade-off reveals where responsibility lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace each technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/1/paragraphs/3: The resilience trade-off is the fourth component of the capstone decision on designing global BlueRetail, but its value becomes clear only in the context of the business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking what a service does. Identify the inputs received by the resilience trade-off, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. BlueRetail needs a global architecture that connects subsidiaries, storefronts, the data platform, and shared services. Connecting the resilience trade-off to the enterprise requirement reveals where responsibility lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace each technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/2/title: Make decisions step by step and document the trade-offs
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving the enterprise requirement should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm the mandatory requirements, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the enterprise requirement with the governance boundary using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 62, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving the global architecture should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm the mandatory requirements, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the global architecture with the resilience trade-off using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 62, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving the governance boundary should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm the mandatory requirements, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the governance boundary with the enterprise requirement using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 62, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving the resilience trade-off should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm the mandatory requirements, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the resilience trade-off with the global architecture using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 62, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would trigger an architecture review.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/3/title: Design for failure before it occurs
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in the capstone on designing global BlueRetail occurs when the project team assumes that the enterprise requirement automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the enterprise requirement, marking the parts for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in the capstone on designing global BlueRetail occurs when the project team assumes that the global architecture automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the global architecture, marking the parts for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in the capstone on designing global BlueRetail occurs when the project team assumes that the governance boundary automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. For BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the governance boundary, marking the parts for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy, but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/4/title: Apply the decision to the BlueRetail case
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 62, BlueRetail needs a global architecture that connects subsidiaries, storefronts, the data platform, and shared services. The first step is to distinguish the stakeholders: users need a consistent experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and product teams need rapid change. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the enterprise requirement, global architecture, and governance boundary. Each option is scored using the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of consumption. The resilience trade-off serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for the capstone on designing global BlueRetail is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations share the same understanding of the decision. In exam questions, the four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that fails a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb in the final sentence. For the capstone on designing global BlueRetail, mark every detail related to the enterprise requirement and global architecture. Then classify each answer as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not select an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute terms such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful evaluation. They may demand a completely different design from terms such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 62, use the governance boundary to validate the assurance level and the resilience trade-off to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer that provides the correct feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 62 — Capstone: designing global BlueRetail, the SAP-C02 Domains 1–2 objectives indicate the required depth. For design, balance quality attributes; for determine, interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architecture decision.

sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the enterprise requirement and the responsibilities of the global architecture.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to the governance boundary.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess the resilience trade-off.

sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 62, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.

sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 62, an option is correct only when the entire chain of responsibilities and failure modes is addressed.

sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./examFocus/0: Read the verbs in the SAP-C02 Domains 1–2 objectives to determine the required depth of the answer.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./examFocus/1: Distinguish mandatory requirements from background details related to the enterprise requirement.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./examFocus/2: Eliminate answers that shift risk from the global architecture to the governance boundary without actually addressing it.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./examFocus/3: Select the option with an appropriate operational burden and evidence through the resilience trade-off.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/title: Architecture review: Capstone: designing global BlueRetail
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because it needs a global architecture that connects subsidiaries, storefronts, the data platform, and shared services. You must review the architecture before the team deploys it or incurs cost commitments.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads, translating requirements into decisions and clearly communicating trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are enterprise requirement → global architecture → governance boundary → resilience trade-off.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the enterprise requirement.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the global architecture.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the governance boundary without relying on assumptions.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal an inadequately defined requirement.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/constraints/1: The resilience trade-off must be accepted by stakeholders.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural justification.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the enterprise requirement, global architecture, and governance boundary.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-deployment evidence.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unvalidated assumptions.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flows, including trust boundaries and blast radius.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow directions.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix comparing at least two options.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the enterprise requirement and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents selecting services based on preference or isolated keywords.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Compares the global architecture, governance boundary, and resilience trade-off using the same criteria.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always involves balancing competing concerns; there is no universally best option.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, metric, or validation test.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only if it can be operated and validated after deployment.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the enterprise requirement; the global architecture is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: The option that prioritizes the governance boundary is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of the resilience trade-off without adding value.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the enterprise requirement and global architecture, clearly documenting the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the need for a global architecture that connects subsidiaries, storefronts, the data platform, and shared services.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to the requirement.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and ignoring failure modes.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/commonMistakes/2: Claiming that “AWS manages it” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reassess the decision?
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./recap/0: The capstone on designing global BlueRetail must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./recap/1: The enterprise requirement, global architecture, governance boundary, and resilience trade-off form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./recap/2: Every decision needs a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that fail mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of the enterprise requirement and global architecture?
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode relates to the governance boundary, and what is the corresponding recovery path?

sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sources/0/label: AWS Exam Guide SAP-C02 · SAP-C02 Domains 1–2
sap-62-enterprise-capstone-design./sources/2/label: GitHub topic inventory used to verify coverage — Lesson 62

sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./summary: Understand the portfolio improvement and modernization capstone through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an explainable architecture decision for the BlueRetail team.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture. The current architecture presents operational risks and must be migrated without disrupting the business program. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 63 starts with this scenario and turns the portfolio improvement and modernization capstone into a sequence of evidence-based decisions rather than a list of features to memorize.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” The portfolio improvement and modernization capstone should be examined from four perspectives: evidence backlog, migration wave, modernization decision, and value measurement. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that conflict with the requirements or introduce unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 63 — Capstone: Portfolio Improvement and Modernization is aligned with the SAP-C02 Domains 3–4 objectives in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to verify coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are original, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options were not chosen.”
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/0/title: Mental Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among the business, application, and platform. The business defines what must remain operational and the acceptable level of loss; the application describes traffic, data, and dependencies; and the platform translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For the portfolio improvement and modernization capstone, the evidence backlog is the entry point, the migration wave shows how the system responds, and the modernization decision and value measurement determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A compact model that applies to every scenario is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architecture structure; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to the portfolio improvement and modernization capstone, learners can see that the same service may be appropriate for one workload but unsuitable for another. This skill is more important than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 63 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and which mechanism provides recovery? If the narrative breaks at the evidence backlog or migration wave, the diagram still contains an unverified assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Be Connected
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The evidence backlog is the 1st component of the portfolio improvement and modernization capstone decision, but it provides value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What does this service do?” Identify the input received by the evidence backlog, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the current architecture presents operational risks and must be migrated without disrupting the business program. Connecting the evidence backlog to the migration wave reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The migration wave is the 2nd component of the portfolio improvement and modernization capstone decision, but it provides value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What does this service do?” Identify the input received by the migration wave, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the current architecture presents operational risks and must be migrated without disrupting the business program. Connecting the migration wave to the modernization decision reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The modernization decision is the 3rd component of the portfolio improvement and modernization capstone decision, but it provides value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What does this service do?” Identify the input received by the modernization decision, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the current architecture presents operational risks and must be migrated without disrupting the business program. Connecting the modernization decision to value measurement reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Value measurement is the 4th component of the portfolio improvement and modernization capstone decision, but it provides value only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should go beyond asking, “What does this service do?” Identify the input received by value measurement, the state or policies it manages, the failure modes it addresses, and the resulting operational cost. At BlueRetail, the current architecture presents operational risks and must be migrated without disrupting the business program. Connecting value measurement to the evidence backlog reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A strong solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Document Trade-offs
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving the evidence backlog should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the evidence backlog with the modernization decision using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 63, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would require the architecture to be reassessed.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving the migration wave should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the migration wave with value measurement using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 63, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would require the architecture to be reassessed.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving the modernization decision should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare the modernization decision with the evidence backlog using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 63, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would require the architecture to be reassessed.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving value measurement should be documented as a chain of reasoning, not as an isolated service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota, Region, consistency, network path, and responsibility-model constraints. Next, compare value measurement with the migration wave using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after implementation. In Lesson 63, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context and which signals would require the architecture to be reassessed.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before It Occurs
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 of the portfolio improvement and modernization capstone occurs when the project team assumes that the evidence backlog automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the evidence backlog and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 of the portfolio improvement and modernization capstone occurs when the project team assumes that the migration wave automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the migration wave and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 of the portfolio improvement and modernization capstone occurs when the project team assumes that the modernization decision automatically addresses every related layer. In practice, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling does not fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, diagram the blast radius of the modernization decision and mark which parts are the responsibility of AWS and which the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide early warning. Analyzing failure modes first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 63, the current architecture presents operational risks and must be migrated without disrupting the business program. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a stable experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on the evidence backlog, migration wave, and modernization decision. Each option is scored using the same matrix: fulfillment of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of usage. Value measurement serves as the final validation criterion. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the constraints must be revisited.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for the portfolio improvement and modernization capstone is written as four statements: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, the four statements also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not meet a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/5/title: Choosing the Right Action in SAP-C02 Questions
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, current state, mandatory outcome, constraints, and the verb at the end of the question. For the portfolio improvement and modernization capstone, mark every detail related to the evidence backlog and migration wave. Then classify each answer as fully compliant, partially compliant, introducing additional risk, or out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may require a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 63, use the modernization decision to verify the assurance level and value measurement to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the correct feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 63 — Capstone: Portfolio Improvement and Modernization, the SAP-C02 Domains 3–4 objectives indicate the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select the cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints and reduce risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architecture decision.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./visuals/0/title: Decision Map: Capstone: Portfolio Improvement and Modernization
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram connecting requirements to the evidence backlog, migration wave, modernization decision, and value measurement in the BlueRetail case.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./decisionWalkthroughs/0/title: Decision Analysis for the Portfolio Improvement and Modernization Capstone
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./decisionWalkthroughs/0/businessRequirem: BlueRetail must address the situation in which “the current architecture presents operational risks and must be migrated without disrupting the business program,” while still being able to explain the impact on security, resiliency, performance, and cost.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./decisionWalkthroughs/0/technicalConstra: The design must clarify the boundary of the evidence backlog and the responsibilities of the migration wave.
The option must include a failure mode and recovery path related to the modernization decision.
The operations team must use real data to observe and reassess value measurement.

sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/0/lim: Does not automatically address the migration wave, and the remaining responsibilities must be verified.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./decisionWalkthroughs/0/candidates/1/fit: Suitable when the requirement prioritizes the migration wave perspective in step 2.

sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./decisionWalkthroughs/0/reasoning/3: Identify the metric or review that would require the decision to be reassessed.

sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./examFocus/0: Read the verbs in the SAP-C02 Domains 3–4 objectives to determine the required depth of the answer.

sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/title: Architecture Review: Capstone: Portfolio Improvement and Modernization
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/context: BlueRetail is preparing to approve a change because the current architecture presents operational risks and must be migrated without disrupting the business program. You must review the architecture before the team implements it or incurs a cost commitment.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/learnerRole: You are the Lead Solutions Architect responsible for multiple accounts and workloads. You must translate requirements into decisions and clearly communicate trade-offs to stakeholders.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/currentArchitecture: Users → access layer → workload → data store; the review points are evidence backlog → migration wave → modernization decision → value measurement.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/requirements/0: The solution must correctly address the requirement related to the evidence backlog.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/requirements/1: The recovery path must demonstrate the role of the migration wave.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/requirements/2: Security and operations must be able to observe the modernization decision without relying on assumptions.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/constraints/0: Do not add a service merely to conceal a requirement that has not been clearly defined.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/constraints/1: Stakeholders must accept the trade-off involving value measurement.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/constraints/2: Do not change the storage schema or account boundary without an architectural reason.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/tasks/0: Rewrite the requirement as measurable criteria.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/tasks/1: Compare at least two options based on the evidence backlog, migration wave, and modernization decision.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/tasks/2: Select an option, document the trade-off, and propose post-implementation evidence.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Separate the business outcome, technical constraints, and unconfirmed assumptions.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Diagram the request and data flows, including trust boundaries and blast radius.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before optimizing.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Compare the remaining options across reliability, security, performance, cost, and operations.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/reasoningSteps/4: Document the decision, trade-off, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/deliverables/0: An architecture diagram showing boundaries and data-flow directions.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/deliverables/1: A decision matrix with at least two options.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/deliverables/2: A concise architecture decision record containing trade-offs and evidence.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/rubric/0/evidence: The decision can be traced back to the evidence backlog and introduces no assumptions beyond the scenario.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/rubric/0/importance: Prevents services from being selected based on preference or an isolated keyword.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/rubric/1/evidence: The migration wave, modernization decision, and value measurement are compared using the same criteria.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/rubric/1/importance: AWS architecture always requires balance; there is no universally best option.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The submission identifies a failure mode, recovery path, and validating metric or test.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/rubric/2/importance: A design is trustworthy only when it can be operated and validated after implementation.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: The primary requirement is rewritten around the evidence backlog; the migration wave is a constraint that directly affects the choice.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: An option prioritizing the modernization decision is eliminated if it increases the operational burden of value measurement without providing additional value.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: The proposed architecture combines the evidence backlog and migration wave and clearly documents the blast radius, recovery path, and review metric.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/sampleAnalysis/3: The remaining trade-off is accepted because it fits the situation in which the current architecture presents operational risks and must be migrated without disrupting the business program.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/commonMistakes/0: Listing services without connecting them to requirements.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/commonMistakes/1: Describing only the happy path and overlooking failure modes.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/commonMistakes/2: Claiming that “AWS manages it automatically” without identifying the customer's responsibilities.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: Which detail in the scenario eliminated the most options?
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: Which metric or event would cause you to reassess the decision?
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./activity/reflectionQuestions/2: If the budget or RTO changes, which part of the architecture must change first?
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./recap/0: The portfolio improvement and modernization capstone must begin with requirements and constraints.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./recap/1: The evidence backlog, migration wave, modernization decision, and value measurement form a chain of responsibilities.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./recap/2: Every decision requires a trade-off, recovery path, and operational evidence.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./recap/3: In exam questions, eliminate options that do not meet mandatory requirements before comparing optimizations.
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./selfCheck/0: Can you explain the difference between the roles of the evidence backlog and migration wave?
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./selfCheck/1: Which failure mode is related to the modernization decision, and what is the corresponding recovery path?
sap-63-improvement-migration-capstone./selfCheck/2: Why can value measurement change the architecture choice?

sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./title: SAP Review: Multi-Constraint Decisions and Error Logs
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./shortTitle: SAP Review: Multi-Constraint Decisions and Error Logs
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./summary: Understand SAP review: multi-constraint decisions and error logs through requirements, constraints, trade-offs, and an architecture decision that can be explained to the BlueRetail team.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./opening/0: BlueRetail is changing its architecture, and learners understand each service but lose points when long questions contain multiple accounts, Regions, stakeholders, and conflicting requirements. A diagram with many AWS icons is not necessarily a good design: what matters is which requirement each component addresses, which failure modes remain, and whether the operations team can manage it. Lesson 64 starts with this situation and turns SAP review: multi-constraint decisions and error logs into an evidence-based decision sequence instead of a list of features to memorize.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./opening/1: In SAP-C02, questions often present several technically feasible options, but only one best matches keywords such as “least operational effort,” “no data loss,” “low cost,” “multiple accounts,” or “no public internet.” SAP Review: Multi-Constraint Decisions and Error Logs should be approached from four perspectives: scenario decomposition, constraint conflict, distractor elimination, and readiness error log. Each perspective eliminates a group of answers that either conflicts with the requirements or introduces unacceptable responsibilities.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./opening/2: The scope of Lesson 64 — SAP Review: Multi-Constraint Decisions and Error Logs is aligned with SAP-C02 Domains 1–4 in the current AWS Exam Guide. The GitHub topic repository is used only to check coverage and content organization; all explanations, cases, and diagrams here are newly written, while the technical conclusions are validated against AWS Documentation and the Well-Architected Framework. After this lesson, you should be able to explain not only “what to choose,” but also “why the other options should not be chosen.”
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/0/title: Mental Model: Start with Requirements, Not Services
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Treat an AWS architecture as a contract among business, application, and platform teams. The business defines what must continue operating and what level of loss is acceptable; the application team describes traffic, data, and dependencies; the platform team translates those requirements into boundaries, services, policies, and observability mechanisms. For SAP review: multi-constraint decisions and error logs, scenario decomposition is the entry point, constraint conflict shows how the system responds, and distractor elimination and readiness error log determine long-term operability. Moving directly from a requirement to a service name often causes one of these four layers to be overlooked.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/0/paragraphs/1: A simple model that works for every problem is R-C-D-E: Requirement, Constraint, Decision, Evidence. A requirement describes the outcome; a constraint limits the scope through factors such as Region, budget, or skills; a decision is the selected architecture; and evidence is a metric, log, test, or document confirming that the decision remains valid. By applying R-C-D-E to SAP Review: Multi-Constraint Decisions and Error Logs, learners will see that the same service may be right for one workload but wrong for another. This skill matters more than memorizing the entire AWS catalog.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/0/paragraphs/2: You have understood Lesson 64 when you can trace a request or operational event from beginning to end. Which boundary does the request cross, which identity is evaluated, where is the data retained, which component can fail, and what mechanism provides recovery? If the story breaks at scenario decomposition or constraint conflict, the diagram still contains an unvalidated assumption. Record that assumption beside the diagram before selecting another service.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Be Connected
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/1/paragraphs/0: scenario decomposition is the first component in decisions for SAP review: multi-constraint decisions and error logs, but its value emerges only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that scenario decomposition receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, learners understand each service but lose points when long questions contain multiple accounts, Regions, stakeholders, and conflicting requirements. Connecting scenario decomposition to constraint conflict reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/1/paragraphs/1: constraint conflict is the second component in decisions for SAP review: multi-constraint decisions and error logs, but its value emerges only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that constraint conflict receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, learners understand each service but lose points when long questions contain multiple accounts, Regions, stakeholders, and conflicting requirements. Connecting constraint conflict to distractor elimination reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. A good solution must trace the technical choice back to latency, availability, security, data, or the operations team's capabilities.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/1/paragraphs/2: distractor elimination is the third component in decisions for SAP review: multi-constraint decisions and error logs, but its value emerges only when considered alongside business requirements. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that distractor elimination receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. At BlueRetail, learners understand each service but lose points when long questions contain multiple accounts, Regions, stakeholders, and conflicting requirements. Connecting distractor elimination to readiness error log reveals where the responsibility boundary lies and why two similarly named options produce different outcomes. For SAP-C02, learners should not stop at asking, “What is this service used for?” Identify the input that readiness error log receives, the state or policy it manages, the failure mode it handles, and the operational cost it introduces. sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/2/title: Make Decisions Step by Step and Record the Trade-offs
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/2/paragraphs/0: A decision involving scenario decomposition should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare scenario decomposition with distractor elimination using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 64, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context while identifying the signals that should trigger an architecture review.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/2/paragraphs/1: A decision involving constraint conflict should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare constraint conflict with readiness error log using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 64, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context while identifying the signals that should trigger an architecture review.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/2/paragraphs/2: A decision involving distractor elimination should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare distractor elimination with scenario decomposition using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 64, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context while identifying the signals that should trigger an architecture review.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/2/paragraphs/3: A decision involving readiness error log should be recorded as a chain of reasoning, not as a standalone service name. First, confirm which requirements are mandatory, then check quota limits, Region, consistency, network path, and the responsibility model. Next, compare readiness error log with constraint conflict using the same criteria; do not compare one feature of option A with the total cost of option B. Finally, state the remaining trade-off and how it will be observed after deployment. In Lesson 64, this approach helps BlueRetail understand why the selected option is good enough in the current context while identifying the signals that should trigger an architecture review.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/3/title: Design for Failure Before It Occurs
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/3/paragraphs/0: Failure mode 1 in SAP review: multi-constraint decisions and error logs occurs when the project team assumes that scenario decomposition automatically handles every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of scenario decomposition, marking the parts for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/3/paragraphs/1: Failure mode 2 in SAP review: multi-constraint decisions and error logs occurs when the project team assumes that constraint conflict automatically handles every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of constraint conflict, marking the parts for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/3/paragraphs/2: Failure mode 3 in SAP review: multi-constraint decisions and error logs occurs when the project team assumes that distractor elimination automatically handles every related layer. In reality, service availability does not replace correct access permissions, replication does not replace backups, encryption does not automatically grant permission to use a key, and autoscaling cannot fix a stateful dependency. At BlueRetail, draw the blast radius of distractor elimination, marking the parts for which AWS is responsible and those that the application team must still configure or test. Then select a metric, log, or event that can provide an early warning. Analyzing the failure mode first ensures that the answer is valid not only while the system is healthy but also provides a recovery path when an AZ, connection, credential, or operational process fails.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/4/title: Apply the Decision to the BlueRetail Case
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/4/paragraphs/0: BlueRetail has stores, an e-commerce website, internal applications, and an operations team working across multiple accounts. In Lesson 64, learners understand each service but lose points when long questions contain multiple accounts, Regions, stakeholders, and conflicting requirements. The first step is to separate the stakeholders: users need a consistent experience, security needs boundaries and an audit trail, finance needs cost attribution, and the product team needs to make changes quickly. A design optimized for only one stakeholder creates operational debt for another.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The architecture team documents three options centered on scenario decomposition, constraint conflict, and distractor elimination. Each option is scored using the same matrix: satisfaction of mandatory requirements, blast radius, recovery path, operational burden, scalability, and cost per unit of consumption. readiness error log serves as the final validation condition. If an option wins only because the team is familiar with it, the decision lacks sufficient evidence and the team must revisit the constraint.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/4/paragraphs/2: The conclusion for SAP review: multi-constraint decisions and error logs is written in four sentences: which structure is selected; which requirements it satisfies; which trade-off is accepted; and which metric or review will trigger reassessment. This format helps developers, security, and operations understand the same decision. In exam questions, these four sentences also help eliminate distractors: an option that does not meet a mandatory requirement is eliminated first, even if it offers many attractive features.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Read an SAP-C02 question in this order: the assigned role, the current state, the mandatory outcome, the constraint, and the verb at the end of the question. For SAP review: multi-constraint decisions and error logs, mark every detail related to scenario decomposition and constraint conflict. Then classify the answers as fully satisfying the requirements, partially satisfying them, introducing additional risk, or being out of scope. Do not choose an answer merely because it mentions the correct service; AWS tests your ability to combine multiple requirements into the most appropriate solution.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/5/paragraphs/1: Absolute phrases such as “no data loss,” “no downtime,” or “all traffic must remain private” require careful examination. They may demand a completely different design from phrases such as “minimize,” “acceptable,” or “prioritize cost.” In Lesson 64, use distractor elimination to verify the assurance level and readiness error log to determine whether the operations team can sustain the option. An answer with the right feature but the wrong assurance level is still a distractor.
sap-64-exam-reasoning-readiness./sections/5/paragraphs/2: In Lesson 64 — SAP Review: Multi-Constraint Decisions and Error Logs, the SAP-C02 Domains 1–4 objectives indicate the required depth. For design, you must balance quality attributes; for determine, you must interpret the facts and select a cause or option; for improve, you must preserve existing constraints while reducing risk through an appropriate change. End each answer with evidence: a metric, log, policy evaluation, recovery test, or cost report. Evidence turns an answer from a guess into a defensible architecture decision.
